(尼崎市)|Q11589609|神崎}}もしくは{{jalink|豊中市|Q467479|豊中}}）で戦勝祈願したとき、{{jalink|猪名川|Q11571867}}上流の能勢の美奴売山（大阪府豊能郡{{jalink|三草山|Q11356995}}）の神が来て、美奴売山の杉の木を切って船を作れば必ず勝利すると告げた。その通りにして勝利を納めた帰途、古代には南に突き出した岬となっていた当地の沖で船が動かなくなり、船上で占いをするとこれは美奴売山の神の意志であるとわかったので、そこに美奴売神を祀ったという。これはある程度史実を伝えている模様で、『{{jalink|住吉大社神代記|Q11381815}}』にも猪名川の女神と{{jalink|武庫川|Q6933569}}の女神が{{jalink|住吉大神}}の気を引くために、互いに競ったことが記されている。『摂津国風土記』逸文に、{{jalink|豊受大神}}が{{jalink|丹波国|Q954511}}に遷座する前は、摂津国{{jalink|稲倉山}}（所在不明）に居たことが記されているが、この稲倉山は猪名川上流域（{{jalink|豊能郡|Q1205516}} ?）にあったのではないかとの説がある。 『{{jalink|延喜式|Q1342448}}』の{{jalink|玄蕃寮|Q11572314}}の項には、特に、{{jalink|新羅|Q28456}}より賓客が来朝したとき、{{jalink|生田神社|Q710086}}で醸した酒を、当地にてふるまったことが見える。{{jalink|難波|Q1075406}}の{{jalink|鴻臚館|Q11675677}}でも酒をふるまっているので、当地で酒をふるまうことは一種の儀式だった。『{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932}}』では「摂津国八部郡 汶売神社」と記載され、小社に列している。 都から現在の{{jalink|大阪|Q11439906}}を経て、西国に船で向かうときの最初の宿泊港であり、西国から都に戻る時、{{jalink|大和|Q13417196}}を意識させる{{jalink|生駒山|Q996259}}などが見え出す地でもあることから、{{jalink|柿本人麻呂|Q380818}}、{{jalink|大伴旅人|Q386287}}、{{jalink|田辺福麻呂|Q11577372}}はじめ、多くの{{jalink|歌人|Q11122954}}によって、{{jalink|和歌|Q737148}}が当地で詠まれた。{{jalink|江戸時代|Q184963}}には、{{jalink|俳人}}の{{jalink|与謝蕪村|Q31523}}も{{jalink|兵庫津||兵庫}}の{{jalink|北風家|Q11404520}}に立ち寄る途中、度々訪れている。{{jalink|西国街道|Q10518413}}（浜街道）に面していたので、様々な遊戯施設が設けられ、陸からも、海からも、参拝者が訪れ、賑やかであった。 明治6年（{{jalink|1873年|Q7757}}）8月に村社に列格し、昭和5年9月に県社に昇格した。昭和20年6月5日、戦災により社殿を焼失し、昭和27年に再建された。 本殿のある丘の下に湧水があったが、{{jalink|阪神・淡路大震災|Q11392882}}で涸れてしまった。 === 縁切りの神 === [[画像:Mirume-Jinja-mae-BusStop-20230504.jpg|thumb|200px|最寄りのバス停名は「'''みるめ'''じんじゃまえ」と読む]] 古来より、この神社の前を、離縁を恐れて花嫁行列は通らない。女神が嫉妬するからだと言われているが、縁切りでも有名で、様々な縁切の方法が伝えられている。 === 「敏馬」の読みについて === 神社としての「敏馬」の読みは「み'''ぬ'''め」であるが、沿線を運行する阪神電鉄バスの「敏馬神社前」「敏馬」停留所の読みは「み'''る'''め」と読む。明治期～大正期の文献には「み'''る'''め」と読まれているもの{{Cite book|和書 |title=最新近畿遊覧案内 |date=1911-07|publisher=奈仁和書房 |pages=阪神電気鉄道線22 |author=奈仁和会 編}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=神戸名所案内 |date=1897-10|publisher=熊谷久栄堂 |page=7 |author=塚脇門蔵 (高城山人)}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=神戸を中心として 前編 |year=1919|publisher=赤西万有堂 |author=安治博道, 福原潜次郎 編}}、「み'''ぬ'''め」とよまれているもの{{Cite book|和書 |title=万葉集略解 ６ |year=1875|publisher=片野東四郎 |page=17}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=神戸名勝案内記 : 附・近傍 |date=1897-04|publisher=日東館 |page=14 |author=鍋島直身 編}}がみられる。 === 脚注 === {{reflist}} === 外部リンク === * {{Instagram|minume_jinja}} * [https://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6301022.html 敏馬神社] - 兵庫県神社庁 {{DEFAULTSORT:みぬめしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Minume Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Minume Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:敏馬神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11499217}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=April 2020}} {{神社 | 名称=Minume Shrine | 画像=[[ファイル:Minume shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{jalink|Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290}}{{jalink|Kobe|Q48320}}{{jalink|Nada-ku|Q1154555}} Iwaya Naka-machi 4-1-8 | 位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} | 社格={{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small)
former {{jalink|prefectural shrine}} | 創建={{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} 1 | 本殿={{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} | 例祭={{jalink|October 10|Q2921}} | 地図 = {{Location map|Kobe|label=Minume Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Minume Shrine''' (みぬめじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Iwaya Naka-machi|Q11474374}}, {{jalink|Nada-ku|Q1154555}}, {{jalink|Kobe|Q48320}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and its former {{jalink|shrine rank|Q10444029}} was prefectural shrine. It stands on a hill north of where {{jalink|National Route 2|Q1131340}} and {{jalink|National Route 43|Q6157520}} run together east-west. About 150 m southeast of {{jalink|Iwaya Station (Hyōgo)|Q6100854|Iwaya Station}} on Hanshin. === Enshrined deity === In old writings, Minume was also written as 汶売, 美奴売, 三犬女, 見宿女, etc. Now the main deity is {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, with {{jalink|Amaterasu|Q455602|Amaterasu Kōtaishin}} and {{jalink|Kumano no Imasu Kami}} as associated deities; until the Edo period it was called “{{jalink|Gozu Tennō}}.” The original deity was Minume-no-Kami (美奴売神 / 敏馬神). From the name, Minume-no-Kami is considered the same as the water deity {{jalink|Mizuhano-me||Mitsuha-no-me-no-Kami}}, now enshrined in the precinct water shrine. There is a well called Akai or Miro-me Shimizu. The view that this goddess gave her name to Minume is considered strongest. Another theory, likely inspired by {{jalink|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Q380818}}’s poem “Passing Minume where seaweed is harvested...,” derives it from miru ({{jalink|Miru seaweed|Q2712208|miru}}), used in making sea-salt. === History === An excerpt of ''Settsu no Kuni Fudoki'' records this shrine’s foundation. When {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} departed for conquest of Silla, she prayed for victory at Kamisaki Matsubara in {{jalink|Kawabe District|Q1042053}} (present {{jalink|Kanzaki (Amagasaki)|Q11589609|Kanzaki}} in {{jalink|Amagasaki|Q213318}} or {{jalink|Toyonaka|Q467479}}). A deity from Minume-yama in Nose upstream on the {{jalink|Inagawa|Q11571867}} told her to cut cedars from Minume-yama (Mikusa-yama in present Toyono District, Osaka) and make ships to ensure victory. On return, ships stopped offshore of this place, then a cape in antiquity; divination revealed it was the will of the Minume-yama deity, so Minume-no-Kami was enshrined here. This appears to preserve some historical truth. The ''{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki|Q11381815}}'' also records the female deities of Inagawa and {{jalink|Muko River|Q6933569}} competing for favor of {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Ōkami}}. Another ''Settsu no Kuni Fudoki'' excerpt says that before {{jalink|Toyouke Ōkami}} moved to {{jalink|Tanba Province|Q954511}}, the deity resided at Inakura-yama in Settsu (location unknown), and one theory places this in the upper Inagawa area ({{jalink|Toyono District|Q1205516}}?). In the {{jalink|Genba-ryō|Q11572314}} section of the ''{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}'', it is noted that when guests from {{jalink|Silla|Q28456}} visited, sake brewed at {{jalink|Ikuta Shrine|Q710086}} was served here. Since sake was also served at the {{jalink|Kōrōkan|Q11675677}} in {{jalink|Naniwa|Q1075406}}, serving sake here was a kind of ritual. In the ''{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}'', it is listed as “Minume Shrine, Yatabe District, Settsu Province,” ranked as a small shrine. Because this was the first overnight harbor when traveling by ship westward from the capital through present {{jalink|Osaka|Q11439906}}, and also where {{jalink|Mount Ikoma|Q996259}} and other views recalling {{jalink|Yamato|Q13417196}} emerged on return from the west, many poets - including {{jalink|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Q380818}}, {{jalink|Ōtomo no Tabito|Q386287}}, and {{jalink|Tanabe no Sakimaro|Q11577372}} - composed {{jalink|waka|Q737148}} here. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, haikai poet {{jalink|Yosa Buson|Q31523}} also visited repeatedly on his way to the Kitakaze house in Hyōgo. Facing the {{jalink|Saigoku Kaidō|Q10518413}} (Hama Kaidō), with various amusements, it was lively with worshippers from land and sea. In August Meiji 6 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}) it was ranked village shrine; in September Shōwa 5 it was promoted to prefectural shrine. On June 5, Shōwa 20, shrine buildings were destroyed in war damage; rebuilt in Shōwa 27. There used to be a spring beneath the hill of the main sanctuary, but it dried up in the {{jalink|Great Hanshin-Awaji Earthquake|Q11392882}}. === God of severing ties === [[画像:Mirume-Jinja-mae-BusStop-20230504.jpg|thumb|200px|Nearest bus stop is read as “'''mirume''' jinja-mae”]] Traditionally, bridal processions do not pass in front of this shrine for fear of divorce. It is said the goddess would be jealous, and the shrine is also known for rites of severing ties. === Reading of “Minume” === As a shrine name, 敏馬 is read “mi'''nu'''me,” but the nearby Hanshin bus stops “Minume Jinja-mae” and “Minume” are read “mi'''ru'''me.” Meiji-Taishō documents show both readings{{Cite book|和書 |title=Latest Kinki Excursion Guide |date=1911-07|publisher=Naniwa Shobō |pages=Hanshin Electric Railway line 22 |author=Edited by Naniwa-kai}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Landmarks |date=1897-10|publisher=Kumagai Kueidō |page=7 |author=Kadokawa Monzō (Takagiyama-jin)}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Centered on Kobe, Part 1 |year=1919|publisher=Akanishi Manyūdō |author=Edited by Harumichi Anji and Senjirō Fukuhara}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Man'yōshū Ryakukai 6 |year=1875|publisher=Katano Tōshirō |page=17}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Scenic Places: with nearby area |date=1897-04|publisher=Nittōkan |page=14 |author=Edited by Naoomi Nabeshima}}. === Notes === {{reflist}} === External links === * {{Instagram|minume_jinja}} * [https://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6301022.html Minume Shrine] - Hyogo Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines {{DEFAULTSORT:みぬめしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Minume Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Minume Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:神戸市の神社]] [[Category:灘区の建築物]] [[Category:摂津国の式内社]] [[Category:兵庫県の旧県社]] [[Category:祇園神社]] [[Category:灘区の歴史]] [[Category:岩屋 (神戸市)]] [[Category:need translation]]

'language': 'ja'} [[JA:敏馬神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11499217}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=April 2020}} {{神社 | 名称=Minume Shrine | 画像=[[ファイル:Minume shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{jalink|Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290}}{{jalink|Kobe|Q48320}}{{jalink|Nada-ku|Q1154555}} Iwaya Naka-machi 4-1-8 | 位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} | 社格={{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small)
former {{jalink|prefectural shrine}} | 創建={{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} 1 | 本殿={{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} | 例祭={{jalink|October 10|Q2921}} | 地図 = {{Location map|Kobe|label=Minume Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Minume Shrine''' (みぬめじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Iwaya Naka-machi|Q11474374}}, {{jalink|Nada-ku|Q1154555}}, {{jalink|Kobe|Q48320}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and its former {{jalink|shrine rank|Q10444029}} was prefectural shrine. It stands on a hill north of where {{jalink|National Route 2|Q1131340}} and {{jalink|National Route 43|Q6157520}} run together east-west. About 150 m southeast of {{jalink|Iwaya Station (Hyōgo)|Q6100854|Iwaya Station}} on Hanshin. === Enshrined deity === In old writings, Minume was also written as 汶売, 美奴売, 三犬女, 見宿女, etc. Now the main deity is {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, with {{jalink|Amaterasu|Q455602|Amaterasu Kōtaishin}} and {{jalink|Kumano no Imasu Kami}} as associated deities; until the Edo period it was called “{{jalink|Gozu Tennō}}.” The original deity was Minume-no-Kami (美奴売神 / 敏馬神). From the name, Minume-no-Kami is considered the same as the water deity {{jalink|Mizuhano-me||Mitsuha-no-me-no-Kami}}, now enshrined in the precinct water shrine. There is a well called Akai or Miro-me Shimizu. The view that this goddess gave her name to Minume is considered strongest. Another theory, likely inspired by {{jalink|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Q380818}}’s poem “Passing Minume where seaweed is harvested...,” derives it from miru ({{jalink|Miru seaweed|Q2712208|miru}}), used in making sea-salt. === History === An excerpt of ''Settsu no Kuni Fudoki'' records this shrine’s foundation. When {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} departed for conquest of Silla, she prayed for victory at Kamisaki Matsubara in {{jalink|Kawabe District|Q1042053}} (present {{jalink|Kanzaki (Amagasaki)|Q11589609|Kanzaki}} in {{jalink|Amagasaki|Q213318}} or {{jalink|Toyonaka|Q467479}}). A deity from Minume-yama in Nose upstream on the {{jalink|Inagawa|Q11571867}} told her to cut cedars from Minume-yama (Mikusa-yama in present Toyono District, Osaka) and make ships to ensure victory. On return, ships stopped offshore of this place, then a cape in antiquity; divination revealed it was the will of the Minume-yama deity, so Minume-no-Kami was enshrined here. This appears to preserve some historical truth. The ''{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki|Q11381815}}'' also records the female deities of Inagawa and {{jalink|Muko River|Q6933569}} competing for favor of {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Ōkami}}. Another ''Settsu no Kuni Fudoki'' excerpt says that before {{jalink|Toyouke Ōkami}} moved to {{jalink|Tanba Province|Q954511}}, the deity resided at Inakura-yama in Settsu (location unknown), and one theory places this in the upper Inagawa area ({{jalink|Toyono District|Q1205516}}?). In the {{jalink|Genba-ryō|Q11572314}} section of the ''{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}'', it is noted that when guests from {{jalink|Silla|Q28456}} visited, sake brewed at {{jalink|Ikuta Shrine|Q710086}} was served here. Since sake was also served at the {{jalink|Kōrōkan|Q11675677}} in {{jalink|Naniwa|Q1075406}}, serving sake here was a kind of ritual. In the ''{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}'', it is listed as “Minume Shrine, Yatabe District, Settsu Province,” ranked as a small shrine. Because this was the first overnight harbor when traveling by ship westward from the capital through present {{jalink|Osaka|Q11439906}}, and also where {{jalink|Mount Ikoma|Q996259}} and other views recalling {{jalink|Yamato|Q13417196}} emerged on return from the west, many poets - including {{jalink|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Q380818}}, {{jalink|Ōtomo no Tabito|Q386287}}, and {{jalink|Tanabe no Sakimaro|Q11577372}} - composed {{jalink|waka|Q737148}} here. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, haikai poet {{jalink|Yosa Buson|Q31523}} also visited repeatedly on his way to the Kitakaze house in Hyōgo. Facing the {{jalink|Saigoku Kaidō|Q10518413}} (Hama Kaidō), with various amusements, it was lively with worshippers from land and sea. In August Meiji 6 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}) it was ranked village shrine; in September Shōwa 5 it was promoted to prefectural shrine. On June 5, Shōwa 20, shrine buildings were destroyed in war damage; rebuilt in Shōwa 27. There used to be a spring beneath the hill of the main sanctuary, but it dried up in the {{jalink|Great Hanshin-Awaji Earthquake|Q11392882}}. === God of severing ties === [[画像:Mirume-Jinja-mae-BusStop-20230504.jpg|thumb|200px|Nearest bus stop is read as “'''mirume''' jinja-mae”]] Traditionally, bridal processions do not pass in front of this shrine for fear of divorce. It is said the goddess would be jealous, and the shrine is also known for rites of severing ties. === Reading of “Minume” === As a shrine name, 敏馬 is read “mi'''nu'''me,” but the nearby Hanshin bus stops “Minume Jinja-mae” and “Minume” are read “mi'''ru'''me.” Meiji-Taishō documents show both readings{{Cite book|和書 |title=Latest Kinki Excursion Guide |date=1911-07|publisher=Naniwa Shobō |pages=Hanshin Electric Railway line 22 |author=Edited by Naniwa-kai}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Landmarks |date=1897-10|publisher=Kumagai Kueidō |page=7 |author=Kadokawa Monzō (Takagiyama-jin)}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Centered on Kobe, Part 1 |year=1919|publisher=Akanishi Manyūdō |author=Edited by Harumichi Anji and Senjirō Fukuhara}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Man'yōshū Ryakukai 6 |year=1875|publisher=Katano Tōshirō |page=17}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Scenic Places: with nearby area |date=1897-04|publisher=Nittōkan |page=14 |author=Edited by Naoomi Nabeshima}}. === Notes === {{reflist}} === External links === * {{Instagram|minume_jinja}} * [https://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6301022.html Minume Shrine] - Hyogo Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines {{DEFAULTSORT:みぬめしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Minume Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Minume Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:神戸市の神社]] [[Category:灘区の建築物]] [[Category:摂津国の式内社]] [[Category:兵庫県の旧県社]] [[Category:祇園神社]] [[Category:灘区の歴史]] [[Category:岩屋 (神戸市)]] [[Category:need translation]]

former {{jalink|prefectural shrine}} | 創建={{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} 1 | 本殿={{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} | 例祭={{jalink|October 10|Q2921}} | 地図 = {{Location map|Kobe|label=Minume Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Minume Shrine''' (みぬめじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Iwaya Naka-machi|Q11474374}}, {{jalink|Nada-ku|Q1154555}}, {{jalink|Kobe|Q48320}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and its former {{jalink|shrine rank|Q10444029}} was prefectural shrine. It stands on a hill north of where {{jalink|National Route 2|Q1131340}} and {{jalink|National Route 43|Q6157520}} run together east-west. About 150 m southeast of {{jalink|Iwaya Station (Hyōgo)|Q6100854|Iwaya Station}} on Hanshin. === Enshrined deity === In old writings, Minume was also written as 汶売, 美奴売, 三犬女, 見宿女, etc. Now the main deity is {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, with {{jalink|Amaterasu|Q455602|Amaterasu Kōtaishin}} and {{jalink|Kumano no Imasu Kami}} as associated deities; until the Edo period it was called “{{jalink|Gozu Tennō}}.” The original deity was Minume-no-Kami (美奴売神 / 敏馬神). From the name, Minume-no-Kami is considered the same as the water deity {{jalink|Mizuhano-me||Mitsuha-no-me-no-Kami}}, now enshrined in the precinct water shrine. There is a well called Akai or Miro-me Shimizu. The view that this goddess gave her name to Minume is considered strongest. Another theory, likely inspired by {{jalink|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Q380818}}’s poem “Passing Minume where seaweed is harvested...,” derives it from miru ({{jalink|Miru seaweed|Q2712208|miru}}), used in making sea-salt. === History === An excerpt of ''Settsu no Kuni Fudoki'' records this shrine’s foundation. When {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} departed for conquest of Silla, she prayed for victory at Kamisaki Matsubara in {{jalink|Kawabe District|Q1042053}} (present {{jalink|Kanzaki (Amagasaki)|Q11589609|Kanzaki}} in {{jalink|Amagasaki|Q213318}} or {{jalink|Toyonaka|Q467479}}). A deity from Minume-yama in Nose upstream on the {{jalink|Inagawa|Q11571867}} told her to cut cedars from Minume-yama (Mikusa-yama in present Toyono District, Osaka) and make ships to ensure victory. On return, ships stopped offshore of this place, then a cape in antiquity; divination revealed it was the will of the Minume-yama deity, so Minume-no-Kami was enshrined here. This appears to preserve some historical truth. The ''{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki|Q11381815}}'' also records the female deities of Inagawa and {{jalink|Muko River|Q6933569}} competing for favor of {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Ōkami}}. Another ''Settsu no Kuni Fudoki'' excerpt says that before {{jalink|Toyouke Ōkami}} moved to {{jalink|Tanba Province|Q954511}}, the deity resided at Inakura-yama in Settsu (location unknown), and one theory places this in the upper Inagawa area ({{jalink|Toyono District|Q1205516}}?). In the {{jalink|Genba-ryō|Q11572314}} section of the ''{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}'', it is noted that when guests from {{jalink|Silla|Q28456}} visited, sake brewed at {{jalink|Ikuta Shrine|Q710086}} was served here. Since sake was also served at the {{jalink|Kōrōkan|Q11675677}} in {{jalink|Naniwa|Q1075406}}, serving sake here was a kind of ritual. In the ''{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}'', it is listed as “Minume Shrine, Yatabe District, Settsu Province,” ranked as a small shrine. Because this was the first overnight harbor when traveling by ship westward from the capital through present {{jalink|Osaka|Q11439906}}, and also where {{jalink|Mount Ikoma|Q996259}} and other views recalling {{jalink|Yamato|Q13417196}} emerged on return from the west, many poets - including {{jalink|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Q380818}}, {{jalink|Ōtomo no Tabito|Q386287}}, and {{jalink|Tanabe no Sakimaro|Q11577372}} - composed {{jalink|waka|Q737148}} here. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, haikai poet {{jalink|Yosa Buson|Q31523}} also visited repeatedly on his way to the Kitakaze house in Hyōgo. Facing the {{jalink|Saigoku Kaidō|Q10518413}} (Hama Kaidō), with various amusements, it was lively with worshippers from land and sea. In August Meiji 6 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}) it was ranked village shrine; in September Shōwa 5 it was promoted to prefectural shrine. On June 5, Shōwa 20, shrine buildings were destroyed in war damage; rebuilt in Shōwa 27. There used to be a spring beneath the hill of the main sanctuary, but it dried up in the {{jalink|Great Hanshin-Awaji Earthquake|Q11392882}}. === God of severing ties === [[画像:Mirume-Jinja-mae-BusStop-20230504.jpg|thumb|200px|Nearest bus stop is read as “'''mirume''' jinja-mae”]] Traditionally, bridal processions do not pass in front of this shrine for fear of divorce. It is said the goddess would be jealous, and the shrine is also known for rites of severing ties. === Reading of “Minume” === As a shrine name, 敏馬 is read “mi'''nu'''me,” but the nearby Hanshin bus stops “Minume Jinja-mae” and “Minume” are read “mi'''ru'''me.” Meiji-Taishō documents show both readings{{Cite book|和書 |title=Latest Kinki Excursion Guide |date=1911-07|publisher=Naniwa Shobō |pages=Hanshin Electric Railway line 22 |author=Edited by Naniwa-kai}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Landmarks |date=1897-10|publisher=Kumagai Kueidō |page=7 |author=Kadokawa Monzō (Takagiyama-jin)}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Centered on Kobe, Part 1 |year=1919|publisher=Akanishi Manyūdō |author=Edited by Harumichi Anji and Senjirō Fukuhara}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Man'yōshū Ryakukai 6 |year=1875|publisher=Katano Tōshirō |page=17}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Scenic Places: with nearby area |date=1897-04|publisher=Nittōkan |page=14 |author=Edited by Naoomi Nabeshima}}. === Notes === {{reflist}} === External links === * {{Instagram|minume_jinja}} * [https://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6301022.html Minume Shrine] - Hyogo Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines {{DEFAULTSORT:みぬめしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Minume Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Minume Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:神戸市の神社]] [[Category:灘区の建築物]] [[Category:摂津国の式内社]] [[Category:兵庫県の旧県社]] [[Category:祇園神社]] [[Category:灘区の歴史]] [[Category:岩屋 (神戸市)]] [[Category:need translation]]

Ollama and DeepSeek were too bad to actually use. see categorization since some of them are very questionable, especially if there was a massive decrease in page size or the {{jawiki| template is minimal or absent on the page

'language': 'ja'} [[JA:敏馬神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11499217}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=April 2020}} {{神社 | 名称=Minume Shrine | 画像=[[ファイル:Minume shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{jalink|Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290}}{{jalink|Kobe|Q48320}}{{jalink|Nada-ku|Q1154555}} Iwaya Naka-machi 4-1-8 | 位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} | 社格={{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small)
former {{jalink|prefectural shrine}} | 創建={{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} 1 | 本殿={{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} | 例祭={{jalink|October 10|Q2921}} | 地図 = {{Location map|Kobe|label=Minume Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Minume Shrine''' (みぬめじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Iwaya Naka-machi|Q11474374}}, {{jalink|Nada-ku|Q1154555}}, {{jalink|Kobe|Q48320}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and its former {{jalink|shrine rank|Q10444029}} was prefectural shrine. It stands on a hill north of where {{jalink|National Route 2|Q1131340}} and {{jalink|National Route 43|Q6157520}} run together east-west. About 150 m southeast of {{jalink|Iwaya Station (Hyōgo)|Q6100854|Iwaya Station}} on Hanshin. === Enshrined deity === In old writings, Minume was also written as 汶売, 美奴売, 三犬女, 見宿女, etc. Now the main deity is {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, with {{jalink|Amaterasu|Q455602|Amaterasu Kōtaishin}} and {{jalink|Kumano no Imasu Kami}} as associated deities; until the Edo period it was called “{{jalink|Gozu Tennō}}.” The original deity was Minume-no-Kami (美奴売神 / 敏馬神). From the name, Minume-no-Kami is considered the same as the water deity {{jalink|Mizuhano-me||Mitsuha-no-me-no-Kami}}, now enshrined in the precinct water shrine. There is a well called Akai or Miro-me Shimizu. The view that this goddess gave her name to Minume is considered strongest. Another theory, likely inspired by {{jalink|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Q380818}}’s poem “Passing Minume where seaweed is harvested...,” derives it from miru ({{jalink|Miru seaweed|Q2712208|miru}}), used in making sea-salt. === History === An excerpt of ''Settsu no Kuni Fudoki'' records this shrine’s foundation. When {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} departed for conquest of Silla, she prayed for victory at Kamisaki Matsubara in {{jalink|Kawabe District|Q1042053}} (present {{jalink|Kanzaki (Amagasaki)|Q11589609|Kanzaki}} in {{jalink|Amagasaki|Q213318}} or {{jalink|Toyonaka|Q467479}}). A deity from Minume-yama in Nose upstream on the {{jalink|Inagawa|Q11571867}} told her to cut cedars from Minume-yama (Mikusa-yama in present Toyono District, Osaka) and make ships to ensure victory. On return, ships stopped offshore of this place, then a cape in antiquity; divination revealed it was the will of the Minume-yama deity, so Minume-no-Kami was enshrined here. This appears to preserve some historical truth. The ''{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki|Q11381815}}'' also records the female deities of Inagawa and {{jalink|Muko River|Q6933569}} competing for favor of {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Ōkami}}. Another ''Settsu no Kuni Fudoki'' excerpt says that before {{jalink|Toyouke Ōkami}} moved to {{jalink|Tanba Province|Q954511}}, the deity resided at Inakura-yama in Settsu (location unknown), and one theory places this in the upper Inagawa area ({{jalink|Toyono District|Q1205516}}?). In the {{jalink|Genba-ryō|Q11572314}} section of the ''{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}'', it is noted that when guests from {{jalink|Silla|Q28456}} visited, sake brewed at {{jalink|Ikuta Shrine|Q710086}} was served here. Since sake was also served at the {{jalink|Kōrōkan|Q11675677}} in {{jalink|Naniwa|Q1075406}}, serving sake here was a kind of ritual. In the ''{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}'', it is listed as “Minume Shrine, Yatabe District, Settsu Province,” ranked as a small shrine. Because this was the first overnight harbor when traveling by ship westward from the capital through present {{jalink|Osaka|Q11439906}}, and also where {{jalink|Mount Ikoma|Q996259}} and other views recalling {{jalink|Yamato|Q13417196}} emerged on return from the west, many poets - including {{jalink|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Q380818}}, {{jalink|Ōtomo no Tabito|Q386287}}, and {{jalink|Tanabe no Sakimaro|Q11577372}} - composed {{jalink|waka|Q737148}} here. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, haikai poet {{jalink|Yosa Buson|Q31523}} also visited repeatedly on his way to the Kitakaze house in Hyōgo. Facing the {{jalink|Saigoku Kaidō|Q10518413}} (Hama Kaidō), with various amusements, it was lively with worshippers from land and sea. In August Meiji 6 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}) it was ranked village shrine; in September Shōwa 5 it was promoted to prefectural shrine. On June 5, Shōwa 20, shrine buildings were destroyed in war damage; rebuilt in Shōwa 27. There used to be a spring beneath the hill of the main sanctuary, but it dried up in the {{jalink|Great Hanshin-Awaji Earthquake|Q11392882}}. === God of severing ties === [[画像:Mirume-Jinja-mae-BusStop-20230504.jpg|thumb|200px|Nearest bus stop is read as “'''mirume''' jinja-mae”]] Traditionally, bridal processions do not pass in front of this shrine for fear of divorce. It is said the goddess would be jealous, and the shrine is also known for rites of severing ties. === Reading of “Minume” === As a shrine name, 敏馬 is read “mi'''nu'''me,” but the nearby Hanshin bus stops “Minume Jinja-mae” and “Minume” are read “mi'''ru'''me.” Meiji-Taishō documents show both readings{{Cite book|和書 |title=Latest Kinki Excursion Guide |date=1911-07|publisher=Naniwa Shobō |pages=Hanshin Electric Railway line 22 |author=Edited by Naniwa-kai}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Landmarks |date=1897-10|publisher=Kumagai Kueidō |page=7 |author=Kadokawa Monzō (Takagiyama-jin)}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Centered on Kobe, Part 1 |year=1919|publisher=Akanishi Manyūdō |author=Edited by Harumichi Anji and Senjirō Fukuhara}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Man'yōshū Ryakukai 6 |year=1875|publisher=Katano Tōshirō |page=17}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Scenic Places: with nearby area |date=1897-04|publisher=Nittōkan |page=14 |author=Edited by Naoomi Nabeshima}}. === Notes === {{reflist}} === External links === * {{Instagram|minume_jinja}} * [https://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6301022.html Minume Shrine] - Hyogo Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines {{DEFAULTSORT:みぬめしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Minume Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Minume Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:神戸市の神社]] [[Category:灘区の建築物]] [[Category:摂津国の式内社]] [[Category:兵庫県の旧県社]] [[Category:祇園神社]] [[Category:灘区の歴史]] [[Category:岩屋 (神戸市)]] [[Category:need translation]]

former {{jalink|prefectural shrine}} | 創建={{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} 1 | 本殿={{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} | 例祭={{jalink|October 10|Q2921}} | 地図 = {{Location map|Kobe|label=Minume Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Minume Shrine''' (みぬめじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Iwaya Naka-machi|Q11474374}}, {{jalink|Nada-ku|Q1154555}}, {{jalink|Kobe|Q48320}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and its former {{jalink|shrine rank|Q10444029}} was prefectural shrine. It stands on a hill north of where {{jalink|National Route 2|Q1131340}} and {{jalink|National Route 43|Q6157520}} run together east-west. About 150 m southeast of {{jalink|Iwaya Station (Hyōgo)|Q6100854|Iwaya Station}} on Hanshin. === Enshrined deity === In old writings, Minume was also written as 汶売, 美奴売, 三犬女, 見宿女, etc. Now the main deity is {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, with {{jalink|Amaterasu|Q455602|Amaterasu Kōtaishin}} and {{jalink|Kumano no Imasu Kami}} as associated deities; until the Edo period it was called “{{jalink|Gozu Tennō}}.” The original deity was Minume-no-Kami (美奴売神 / 敏馬神). From the name, Minume-no-Kami is considered the same as the water deity {{jalink|Mizuhano-me||Mitsuha-no-me-no-Kami}}, now enshrined in the precinct water shrine. There is a well called Akai or Miro-me Shimizu. The view that this goddess gave her name to Minume is considered strongest. Another theory, likely inspired by {{jalink|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Q380818}}’s poem “Passing Minume where seaweed is harvested...,” derives it from miru ({{jalink|Miru seaweed|Q2712208|miru}}), used in making sea-salt. === History === An excerpt of ''Settsu no Kuni Fudoki'' records this shrine’s foundation. When {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} departed for conquest of Silla, she prayed for victory at Kamisaki Matsubara in {{jalink|Kawabe District|Q1042053}} (present {{jalink|Kanzaki (Amagasaki)|Q11589609|Kanzaki}} in {{jalink|Amagasaki|Q213318}} or {{jalink|Toyonaka|Q467479}}). A deity from Minume-yama in Nose upstream on the {{jalink|Inagawa|Q11571867}} told her to cut cedars from Minume-yama (Mikusa-yama in present Toyono District, Osaka) and make ships to ensure victory. On return, ships stopped offshore of this place, then a cape in antiquity; divination revealed it was the will of the Minume-yama deity, so Minume-no-Kami was enshrined here. This appears to preserve some historical truth. The ''{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki|Q11381815}}'' also records the female deities of Inagawa and {{jalink|Muko River|Q6933569}} competing for favor of {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Ōkami}}. Another ''Settsu no Kuni Fudoki'' excerpt says that before {{jalink|Toyouke Ōkami}} moved to {{jalink|Tanba Province|Q954511}}, the deity resided at Inakura-yama in Settsu (location unknown), and one theory places this in the upper Inagawa area ({{jalink|Toyono District|Q1205516}}?). In the {{jalink|Genba-ryō|Q11572314}} section of the ''{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}'', it is noted that when guests from {{jalink|Silla|Q28456}} visited, sake brewed at {{jalink|Ikuta Shrine|Q710086}} was served here. Since sake was also served at the {{jalink|Kōrōkan|Q11675677}} in {{jalink|Naniwa|Q1075406}}, serving sake here was a kind of ritual. In the ''{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}'', it is listed as “Minume Shrine, Yatabe District, Settsu Province,” ranked as a small shrine. Because this was the first overnight harbor when traveling by ship westward from the capital through present {{jalink|Osaka|Q11439906}}, and also where {{jalink|Mount Ikoma|Q996259}} and other views recalling {{jalink|Yamato|Q13417196}} emerged on return from the west, many poets - including {{jalink|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Q380818}}, {{jalink|Ōtomo no Tabito|Q386287}}, and {{jalink|Tanabe no Sakimaro|Q11577372}} - composed {{jalink|waka|Q737148}} here. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, haikai poet {{jalink|Yosa Buson|Q31523}} also visited repeatedly on his way to the Kitakaze house in Hyōgo. Facing the {{jalink|Saigoku Kaidō|Q10518413}} (Hama Kaidō), with various amusements, it was lively with worshippers from land and sea. In August Meiji 6 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}) it was ranked village shrine; in September Shōwa 5 it was promoted to prefectural shrine. On June 5, Shōwa 20, shrine buildings were destroyed in war damage; rebuilt in Shōwa 27. There used to be a spring beneath the hill of the main sanctuary, but it dried up in the {{jalink|Great Hanshin-Awaji Earthquake|Q11392882}}. === God of severing ties === [[画像:Mirume-Jinja-mae-BusStop-20230504.jpg|thumb|200px|Nearest bus stop is read as “'''mirume''' jinja-mae”]] Traditionally, bridal processions do not pass in front of this shrine for fear of divorce. It is said the goddess would be jealous, and the shrine is also known for rites of severing ties. === Reading of “Minume” === As a shrine name, 敏馬 is read “mi'''nu'''me,” but the nearby Hanshin bus stops “Minume Jinja-mae” and “Minume” are read “mi'''ru'''me.” Meiji-Taishō documents show both readings{{Cite book|和書 |title=Latest Kinki Excursion Guide |date=1911-07|publisher=Naniwa Shobō |pages=Hanshin Electric Railway line 22 |author=Edited by Naniwa-kai}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Landmarks |date=1897-10|publisher=Kumagai Kueidō |page=7 |author=Kadokawa Monzō (Takagiyama-jin)}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Centered on Kobe, Part 1 |year=1919|publisher=Akanishi Manyūdō |author=Edited by Harumichi Anji and Senjirō Fukuhara}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Man'yōshū Ryakukai 6 |year=1875|publisher=Katano Tōshirō |page=17}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Scenic Places: with nearby area |date=1897-04|publisher=Nittōkan |page=14 |author=Edited by Naoomi Nabeshima}}. === Notes === {{reflist}} === External links === * {{Instagram|minume_jinja}} * [https://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6301022.html Minume Shrine] - Hyogo Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines {{DEFAULTSORT:みぬめしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Minume Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Minume Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:神戸市の神社]] [[Category:灘区の建築物]] [[Category:摂津国の式内社]] [[Category:兵庫県の旧県社]] [[Category:祇園神社]] [[Category:灘区の歴史]] [[Category:岩屋 (神戸市)]] [[Category:need translation]]

There used to be a spring beneath the hill of the main sanctuary, but it dried up in the {{jalink|Great Hanshin-Awaji Earthquake|Q11392882}}. === God of severing ties === [[画像:Mirume-Jinja-mae-BusStop-20230504.jpg|thumb|200px|Nearest bus stop is read as “'''mirume''' jinja-mae”]] Traditionally, bridal processions do not pass in front of this shrine for fear of divorce. It is said the goddess would be jealous, and the shrine is also known for rites of severing ties. === Reading of “Minume” === As a shrine name, 敏馬 is read “mi'''nu'''me,” but the nearby Hanshin bus stops “Minume Jinja-mae” and “Minume” are read “mi'''ru'''me.” Meiji-Taishō documents show both readings{{Cite book|和書 |title=Latest Kinki Excursion Guide |date=1911-07|publisher=Naniwa Shobō |pages=Hanshin Electric Railway line 22 |author=Edited by Naniwa-kai}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Landmarks |date=1897-10|publisher=Kumagai Kueidō |page=7 |author=Kadokawa Monzō (Takagiyama-jin)}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Centered on Kobe, Part 1 |year=1919|publisher=Akanishi Manyūdō |author=Edited by Harumichi Anji and Senjirō Fukuhara}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Man'yōshū Ryakukai 6 |year=1875|publisher=Katano Tōshirō |page=17}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Scenic Places: with nearby area |date=1897-04|publisher=Nittōkan |page=14 |author=Edited by Naoomi Nabeshima}}. === Notes === {{reflist}} === External links === * {{Instagram|minume_jinja}} * [https://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6301022.html Minume Shrine] - Hyogo Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines {{DEFAULTSORT:みぬめしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Minume Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Minume Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:神戸市の神社]] [[Category:灘区の建築物]] [[Category:摂津国の式内社]] [[Category:兵庫県の旧県社]] [[Category:祇園神社]] [[Category:灘区の歴史]] [[Category:岩屋 (神戸市)]]

'language': 'ja'} [[JA:敏馬神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11499217}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=April 2020}} {{神社 | 名称=Minume Shrine | 画像=[[ファイル:Minume shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{jalink|Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290}}{{jalink|Kobe|Q48320}}{{jalink|Nada-ku|Q1154555}} Iwaya Naka-machi 4-1-8 | 位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} | 社格={{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small)
former {{jalink|prefectural shrine}} | 創建={{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} 1 | 本殿={{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} | 例祭={{jalink|October 10|Q2921}} | 地図 = {{Location map|Kobe|label=Minume Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Minume Shrine''' (みぬめじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Iwaya Naka-machi|Q11474374}}, {{jalink|Nada-ku|Q1154555}}, {{jalink|Kobe|Q48320}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and its former {{jalink|shrine rank|Q10444029}} was prefectural shrine. It stands on a hill north of where {{jalink|National Route 2|Q1131340}} and {{jalink|National Route 43|Q6157520}} run together east-west. About 150 m southeast of {{jalink|Iwaya Station (Hyōgo)|Q6100854|Iwaya Station}} on Hanshin. === Enshrined deity === In old writings, Minume was also written as 汶売, 美奴売, 三犬女, 見宿女, etc. Now the main deity is {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, with {{jalink|Amaterasu|Q455602|Amaterasu Kōtaishin}} and {{jalink|Kumano no Imasu Kami}} as associated deities; until the Edo period it was called “{{jalink|Gozu Tennō}}.” The original deity was Minume-no-Kami (美奴売神 / 敏馬神). From the name, Minume-no-Kami is considered the same as the water deity {{jalink|Mizuhano-me||Mitsuha-no-me-no-Kami}}, now enshrined in the precinct water shrine. There is a well called Akai or Miro-me Shimizu. The view that this goddess gave her name to Minume is considered strongest. Another theory, likely inspired by {{jalink|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Q380818}}’s poem “Passing Minume where seaweed is harvested...,” derives it from miru ({{jalink|Miru seaweed|Q2712208|miru}}), used in making sea-salt. === History === An excerpt of ''Settsu no Kuni Fudoki'' records this shrine’s foundation. When {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} departed for conquest of Silla, she prayed for victory at Kamisaki Matsubara in {{jalink|Kawabe District|Q1042053}} (present {{jalink|Kanzaki (Amagasaki)|Q11589609|Kanzaki}} in {{jalink|Amagasaki|Q213318}} or {{jalink|Toyonaka|Q467479}}). A deity from Minume-yama in Nose upstream on the {{jalink|Inagawa|Q11571867}} told her to cut cedars from Minume-yama (Mikusa-yama in present Toyono District, Osaka) and make ships to ensure victory. On return, ships stopped offshore of this place, then a cape in antiquity; divination revealed it was the will of the Minume-yama deity, so Minume-no-Kami was enshrined here. This appears to preserve some historical truth. The ''{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki|Q11381815}}'' also records the female deities of Inagawa and {{jalink|Muko River|Q6933569}} competing for favor of {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Ōkami}}. Another ''Settsu no Kuni Fudoki'' excerpt says that before {{jalink|Toyouke Ōkami}} moved to {{jalink|Tanba Province|Q954511}}, the deity resided at Inakura-yama in Settsu (location unknown), and one theory places this in the upper Inagawa area ({{jalink|Toyono District|Q1205516}}?). In the {{jalink|Genba-ryō|Q11572314}} section of the ''{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}'', it is noted that when guests from {{jalink|Silla|Q28456}} visited, sake brewed at {{jalink|Ikuta Shrine|Q710086}} was served here. Since sake was also served at the {{jalink|Kōrōkan|Q11675677}} in {{jalink|Naniwa|Q1075406}}, serving sake here was a kind of ritual. In the ''{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}'', it is listed as “Minume Shrine, Yatabe District, Settsu Province,” ranked as a small shrine. Because this was the first overnight harbor when traveling by ship westward from the capital through present {{jalink|Osaka|Q11439906}}, and also where {{jalink|Mount Ikoma|Q996259}} and other views recalling {{jalink|Yamato|Q13417196}} emerged on return from the west, many poets - including {{jalink|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Q380818}}, {{jalink|Ōtomo no Tabito|Q386287}}, and {{jalink|Tanabe no Sakimaro|Q11577372}} - composed {{jalink|waka|Q737148}} here. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, haikai poet {{jalink|Yosa Buson|Q31523}} also visited repeatedly on his way to the Kitakaze house in Hyōgo. Facing the {{jalink|Saigoku Kaidō|Q10518413}} (Hama Kaidō), with various amusements, it was lively with worshippers from land and sea. In August Meiji 6 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}) it was ranked village shrine; in September Shōwa 5 it was promoted to prefectural shrine. On June 5, Shōwa 20, shrine buildings were destroyed in war damage; rebuilt in Shōwa 27. There used to be a spring beneath the hill of the main sanctuary, but it dried up in the {{jalink|Great Hanshin-Awaji Earthquake|Q11392882}}. === God of severing ties === [[画像:Mirume-Jinja-mae-BusStop-20230504.jpg|thumb|200px|Nearest bus stop is read as “'''mirume''' jinja-mae”]] Traditionally, bridal processions do not pass in front of this shrine for fear of divorce. It is said the goddess would be jealous, and the shrine is also known for rites of severing ties. === Reading of “Minume” === As a shrine name, 敏馬 is read “mi'''nu'''me,” but the nearby Hanshin bus stops “Minume Jinja-mae” and “Minume” are read “mi'''ru'''me.” Meiji-Taishō documents show both readings{{Cite book|和書 |title=Latest Kinki Excursion Guide |date=1911-07|publisher=Naniwa Shobō |pages=Hanshin Electric Railway line 22 |author=Edited by Naniwa-kai}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Landmarks |date=1897-10|publisher=Kumagai Kueidō |page=7 |author=Kadokawa Monzō (Takagiyama-jin)}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Centered on Kobe, Part 1 |year=1919|publisher=Akanishi Manyūdō |author=Edited by Harumichi Anji and Senjirō Fukuhara}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Man'yōshū Ryakukai 6 |year=1875|publisher=Katano Tōshirō |page=17}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Scenic Places: with nearby area |date=1897-04|publisher=Nittōkan |page=14 |author=Edited by Naoomi Nabeshima}}. === Notes === {{reflist}} === External links === * {{Instagram|minume_jinja}} * [https://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6301022.html Minume Shrine] - Hyogo Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines {{DEFAULTSORT:みぬめしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Minume Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Minume Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:神戸市の神社]] [[Category:灘区の建築物]] [[Category:摂津国の式内社]] [[Category:兵庫県の旧県社]] [[Category:祇園神社]] [[Category:灘区の歴史]] [[Category:岩屋 (神戸市)]][[Category:qq]]

'language': 'ja'} [[JA:敏馬神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11499217}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=April 2020}} {{神社 | 名称=Minume Shrine | 画像=[[ファイル:Minume shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{jalink|Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290}}{{jalink|Kobe|Q48320}}{{jalink|Nada-ku|Q1154555}} Iwaya Naka-machi 4-1-8 | 位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} | 社格={{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small)
former {{jalink|prefectural shrine}} | 創建={{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} 1 | 本殿={{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} | 例祭={{jalink|October 10|Q2921}} | 地図 = {{Location map|Kobe|label=Minume Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Minume Shrine''' (みぬめじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Iwaya Naka-machi|Q11474374}}, {{jalink|Nada-ku|Q1154555}}, {{jalink|Kobe|Q48320}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and its former {{jalink|shrine rank|Q10444029}} was prefectural shrine. It stands on a hill north of where {{jalink|National Route 2|Q1131340}} and {{jalink|National Route 43|Q6157520}} run together east-west. About 150 m southeast of {{jalink|Iwaya Station (Hyōgo)|Q6100854|Iwaya Station}} on Hanshin. === Enshrined deity === In old writings, Minume was also written as 汶売, 美奴売, 三犬女, 見宿女, etc. Now the main deity is {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, with {{jalink|Amaterasu|Q455602|Amaterasu Kōtaishin}} and {{jalink|Kumano no Imasu Kami}} as associated deities; until the Edo period it was called “{{jalink|Gozu Tennō}}.” The original deity was Minume-no-Kami (美奴売神 / 敏馬神). From the name, Minume-no-Kami is considered the same as the water deity {{jalink|Mizuhano-me||Mitsuha-no-me-no-Kami}}, now enshrined in the precinct water shrine. There is a well called Akai or Miro-me Shimizu. The view that this goddess gave her name to Minume is considered strongest. Another theory, likely inspired by {{jalink|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Q380818}}’s poem “Passing Minume where seaweed is harvested...,” derives it from miru ({{jalink|Miru seaweed|Q2712208|miru}}), used in making sea-salt. === History === An excerpt of ''Settsu no Kuni Fudoki'' records this shrine’s foundation. When {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} departed for conquest of Silla, she prayed for victory at Kamisaki Matsubara in {{jalink|Kawabe District|Q1042053}} (present {{jalink|Kanzaki (Amagasaki)|Q11589609|Kanzaki}} in {{jalink|Amagasaki|Q213318}} or {{jalink|Toyonaka|Q467479}}). A deity from Minume-yama in Nose upstream on the {{jalink|Inagawa|Q11571867}} told her to cut cedars from Minume-yama (Mikusa-yama in present Toyono District, Osaka) and make ships to ensure victory. On return, ships stopped offshore of this place, then a cape in antiquity; divination revealed it was the will of the Minume-yama deity, so Minume-no-Kami was enshrined here. This appears to preserve some historical truth. The ''{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki|Q11381815}}'' also records the female deities of Inagawa and {{jalink|Muko River|Q6933569}} competing for favor of {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Ōkami}}. Another ''Settsu no Kuni Fudoki'' excerpt says that before {{jalink|Toyouke Ōkami}} moved to {{jalink|Tanba Province|Q954511}}, the deity resided at Inakura-yama in Settsu (location unknown), and one theory places this in the upper Inagawa area ({{jalink|Toyono District|Q1205516}}?). In the {{jalink|Genba-ryō|Q11572314}} section of the ''{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}'', it is noted that when guests from {{jalink|Silla|Q28456}} visited, sake brewed at {{jalink|Ikuta Shrine|Q710086}} was served here. Since sake was also served at the {{jalink|Kōrōkan|Q11675677}} in {{jalink|Naniwa|Q1075406}}, serving sake here was a kind of ritual. In the ''{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}'', it is listed as “Minume Shrine, Yatabe District, Settsu Province,” ranked as a small shrine. Because this was the first overnight harbor when traveling by ship westward from the capital through present {{jalink|Osaka|Q11439906}}, and also where {{jalink|Mount Ikoma|Q996259}} and other views recalling {{jalink|Yamato|Q13417196}} emerged on return from the west, many poets - including {{jalink|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Q380818}}, {{jalink|Ōtomo no Tabito|Q386287}}, and {{jalink|Tanabe no Sakimaro|Q11577372}} - composed {{jalink|waka|Q737148}} here. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, haikai poet {{jalink|Yosa Buson|Q31523}} also visited repeatedly on his way to the Kitakaze house in Hyōgo. Facing the {{jalink|Saigoku Kaidō|Q10518413}} (Hama Kaidō), with various amusements, it was lively with worshippers from land and sea. In August Meiji 6 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}) it was ranked village shrine; in September Shōwa 5 it was promoted to prefectural shrine. On June 5, Shōwa 20, shrine buildings were destroyed in war damage; rebuilt in Shōwa 27. There used to be a spring beneath the hill of the main sanctuary, but it dried up in the {{jalink|Great Hanshin-Awaji Earthquake|Q11392882}}. === God of severing ties === [[画像:Mirume-Jinja-mae-BusStop-20230504.jpg|thumb|200px|Nearest bus stop is read as “'''mirume''' jinja-mae”]] Traditionally, bridal processions do not pass in front of this shrine for fear of divorce. It is said the goddess would be jealous, and the shrine is also known for rites of severing ties. === Reading of “Minume” === As a shrine name, 敏馬 is read “mi'''nu'''me,” but the nearby Hanshin bus stops “Minume Jinja-mae” and “Minume” are read “mi'''ru'''me.” Meiji-Taishō documents show both readings{{Cite book|和書 |title=Latest Kinki Excursion Guide |date=1911-07|publisher=Naniwa Shobō |pages=Hanshin Electric Railway line 22 |author=Edited by Naniwa-kai}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Landmarks |date=1897-10|publisher=Kumagai Kueidō |page=7 |author=Kadokawa Monzō (Takagiyama-jin)}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Centered on Kobe, Part 1 |year=1919|publisher=Akanishi Manyūdō |author=Edited by Harumichi Anji and Senjirō Fukuhara}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Man'yōshū Ryakukai 6 |year=1875|publisher=Katano Tōshirō |page=17}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Scenic Places: with nearby area |date=1897-04|publisher=Nittōkan |page=14 |author=Edited by Naoomi Nabeshima}}. === Notes === {{reflist}} === External links === * {{Instagram|minume_jinja}} * [https://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6301022.html Minume Shrine] - Hyogo Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines {{DEFAULTSORT:みぬめしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Minume Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Minume Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:神戸市の神社]] [[Category:灘区の建築物]] [[Category:摂津国の式内社]] [[Category:兵庫県の旧県社]] [[Category:祇園神社]] [[Category:灘区の歴史]] [[Category:岩屋 (神戸市)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

In August Meiji 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}) it was ranked village shrine; in September Shōwa 5 it was promoted to prefectural shrine. On June 5, Shōwa 20, shrine buildings were destroyed in war damage; rebuilt in Shōwa 27. There used to be a spring beneath the hill of the main sanctuary, but it dried up in the {{ill|lt=Great Hanshin-Awaji Earthquake|Q11392882|WD=Q11392882}}. === God of severing ties === [[画像:Mirume-Jinja-mae-BusStop-20230504.jpg|thumb|200px|Nearest bus stop is read as “'''mirume''' jinja-mae”]] Traditionally, bridal processions do not pass in front of this shrine for fear of divorce. It is said the goddess would be jealous, and the shrine is also known for rites of severing ties. === Reading of “Minume” === As a shrine name, 敏馬 is read “mi'''nu'''me,” but the nearby Hanshin bus stops “Minume Jinja-mae” and “Minume” are read “mi'''ru'''me.” Meiji-Taishō documents show both readings{{Cite book|和書 |title=Latest Kinki Excursion Guide |date=1911-07|publisher=Naniwa Shobō |pages=Hanshin Electric Railway line 22 |author=Edited by Naniwa-kai}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Landmarks |date=1897-10|publisher=Kumagai Kueidō |page=7 |author=Kadokawa Monzō (Takagiyama-jin)}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Centered on Kobe, Part 1 |year=1919|publisher=Akanishi Manyūdō |author=Edited by Harumichi Anji and Senjirō Fukuhara}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Man'yōshū Ryakukai 6 |year=1875|publisher=Katano Tōshirō |page=17}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Scenic Places: with nearby area |date=1897-04|publisher=Nittōkan |page=14 |author=Edited by Naoomi Nabeshima}}. === Notes === {{reflist}} === External links === * {{Instagram|minume_jinja}} * [https://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6301022.html Minume Shrine] - Hyogo Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines {{DEFAULTSORT:みぬめしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Minume Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Minume Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:神戸市の神社]] [[Category:灘区の建築物]] [[Category:摂津国の式内社]] [[Category:兵庫県の旧県社]] [[Category:祇園神社]] [[Category:灘区の歴史]] [[Category:岩屋 (神戸市)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

'language': 'ja'} [[JA:敏馬神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11499217}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=April 2020}} {{神社 | 名称=Minume Shrine | 画像=[[ファイル:Minume shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290}}{{ill|lt=Kobe|Q48320|WD=Q48320}}{{ill|lt=Nada-ku|Q1154555|WD=Q1154555}} Iwaya Naka-machi 4-1-8 | 位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} (small)
former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|UNKNOWN}} | 創建={{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803}} 1 | 本殿={{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} | 例祭={{ill|lt=October 10|Q2921|WD=Q2921}} | 地図 = {{Location map|Kobe|label=Minume Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Minume Shrine''' (みぬめじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Iwaya Naka-machi|Q11474374|WD=Q11474374}}, {{ill|lt=Nada-ku|Q1154555|WD=Q1154555}}, {{ill|lt=Kobe|Q48320|WD=Q48320}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029}} was prefectural shrine. It stands on a hill north of where {{ill|lt=National Route 2|Q1131340|WD=Q1131340}} and {{ill|lt=National Route 43|Q6157520|WD=Q6157520}} run together east-west. About 150 m southeast of {{ill|lt=Iwaya Station (Hyōgo)|Q6100854|WD=Q6100854|lt=Iwaya Station}} on Hanshin. === Enshrined deity === In old writings, Minume was also written as 汶売, 美奴売, 三犬女, 見宿女, etc. Now the main deity is {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, with {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Kōtaishin}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kumano no Imasu Kami|UNKNOWN}} as associated deities; until the Edo period it was called “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gozu Tennō|UNKNOWN}}.” The original deity was Minume-no-Kami (美奴売神 / 敏馬神). From the name, Minume-no-Kami is considered the same as the water deity {{ill|lt=Mizuhano-me||WD=|lt=Mitsuha-no-me-no-Kami}}, now enshrined in the precinct water shrine. There is a well called Akai or Miro-me Shimizu. The view that this goddess gave her name to Minume is considered strongest. Another theory, likely inspired by {{ill|lt=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Q380818|WD=Q380818}}’s poem “Passing Minume where seaweed is harvested...,” derives it from miru ({{ill|lt=Miru seaweed|Q2712208|WD=Q2712208|lt=miru}}), used in making sea-salt. === History === An excerpt of ''Settsu no Kuni Fudoki'' records this shrine’s foundation. When {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803}} departed for conquest of Silla, she prayed for victory at Kamisaki Matsubara in {{ill|lt=Kawabe District|Q1042053|WD=Q1042053}} (present {{ill|lt=Kanzaki (Amagasaki)|Q11589609|WD=Q11589609|lt=Kanzaki}} in {{ill|lt=Amagasaki|Q213318|WD=Q213318}} or {{ill|lt=Toyonaka|Q467479|WD=Q467479}}). A deity from Minume-yama in Nose upstream on the {{ill|lt=Inagawa|Q11571867|WD=Q11571867}} told her to cut cedars from Minume-yama (Mikusa-yama in present Toyono District, Osaka) and make ships to ensure victory. On return, ships stopped offshore of this place, then a cape in antiquity; divination revealed it was the will of the Minume-yama deity, so Minume-no-Kami was enshrined here. This appears to preserve some historical truth. The ''{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki|Q11381815|WD=Q11381815}}'' also records the female deities of Inagawa and {{ill|lt=Muko River|Q6933569|WD=Q6933569}} competing for favor of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sumiyoshi Ōkami|UNKNOWN}}. Another ''Settsu no Kuni Fudoki'' excerpt says that before {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Toyouke Ōkami|UNKNOWN}} moved to {{ill|lt=Tanba Province|Q954511|WD=Q954511}}, the deity resided at Inakura-yama in Settsu (location unknown), and one theory places this in the upper Inagawa area ({{ill|lt=Toyono District|Q1205516|WD=Q1205516}}?). In the {{ill|lt=Genba-ryō|Q11572314|WD=Q11572314}} section of the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}'', it is noted that when guests from {{ill|lt=Silla|Q28456|WD=Q28456}} visited, sake brewed at {{ill|lt=Ikuta Shrine|Q710086|WD=Q710086}} was served here. Since sake was also served at the {{ill|lt=Kōrōkan|Q11675677|WD=Q11675677}} in {{ill|lt=Naniwa|Q1075406|WD=Q1075406}}, serving sake here was a kind of ritual. In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}'', it is listed as “Minume Shrine, Yatabe District, Settsu Province,” ranked as a small shrine. Because this was the first overnight harbor when traveling by ship westward from the capital through present {{ill|lt=Osaka|Q11439906|WD=Q11439906}}, and also where {{ill|lt=Mount Ikoma|Q996259|WD=Q996259}} and other views recalling {{ill|lt=Yamato|Q13417196|WD=Q13417196}} emerged on return from the west, many poets - including {{ill|lt=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Q380818|WD=Q380818}}, {{ill|lt=Ōtomo no Tabito|Q386287|WD=Q386287}}, and {{ill|lt=Tanabe no Sakimaro|Q11577372|WD=Q11577372}} - composed {{ill|lt=waka|Q737148|WD=Q737148}} here. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, haikai poet {{ill|lt=Yosa Buson|Q31523|WD=Q31523}} also visited repeatedly on his way to the Kitakaze house in Hyōgo. Facing the {{ill|lt=Saigoku Kaidō|Q10518413|WD=Q10518413}} (Hama Kaidō), with various amusements, it was lively with worshippers from land and sea. In August Meiji 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}) it was ranked village shrine; in September Shōwa 5 it was promoted to prefectural shrine. On June 5, Shōwa 20, shrine buildings were destroyed in war damage; rebuilt in Shōwa 27. There used to be a spring beneath the hill of the main sanctuary, but it dried up in the {{ill|lt=Great Hanshin-Awaji Earthquake|Q11392882|WD=Q11392882}}. === God of severing ties === [[画像:Mirume-Jinja-mae-BusStop-20230504.jpg|thumb|200px|Nearest bus stop is read as “'''mirume''' jinja-mae”]] Traditionally, bridal processions do not pass in front of this shrine for fear of divorce. It is said the goddess would be jealous, and the shrine is also known for rites of severing ties. === Reading of “Minume” === As a shrine name, 敏馬 is read “mi'''nu'''me,” but the nearby Hanshin bus stops “Minume Jinja-mae” and “Minume” are read “mi'''ru'''me.” Meiji-Taishō documents show both readings{{Cite book|和書 |title=Latest Kinki Excursion Guide |date=1911-07|publisher=Naniwa Shobō |pages=Hanshin Electric Railway line 22 |author=Edited by Naniwa-kai}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Landmarks |date=1897-10|publisher=Kumagai Kueidō |page=7 |author=Kadokawa Monzō (Takagiyama-jin)}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Centered on Kobe, Part 1 |year=1919|publisher=Akanishi Manyūdō |author=Edited by Harumichi Anji and Senjirō Fukuhara}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Man'yōshū Ryakukai 6 |year=1875|publisher=Katano Tōshirō |page=17}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Scenic Places: with nearby area |date=1897-04|publisher=Nittōkan |page=14 |author=Edited by Naoomi Nabeshima}}. === Notes === {{reflist}} === External links === * {{Instagram|minume_jinja}} * [https://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6301022.html Minume Shrine] - Hyogo Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines {{DEFAULTSORT:みぬめしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Minume Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Minume Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:神戸市の神社]] [[Category:灘区の建築物]] [[Category:摂津国の式内社]] [[Category:兵庫県の旧県社]] [[Category:祇園神社]] [[Category:灘区の歴史]] [[Category:岩屋 (神戸市)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

'language': 'ja'} [[JA:敏馬神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11499217}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=April 2020}} {{神社 | 名称=Minume Shrine | 画像=[[ファイル:Minume shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290}}{{ill|lt=Kobe|Q48320|WD=Q48320}}{{ill|lt=Nada-ku|Q1154555|WD=Q1154555}} Iwaya Naka-machi 4-1-8 | 位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}} (small)
former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} | 創建={{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803}} 1 | 本殿={{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} | 例祭={{ill|lt=October 10|Q2921|WD=Q2921}} | 地図 = {{Location map|Kobe|label=Minume Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Minume Shrine''' (みぬめじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Iwaya Naka-machi|Q11474374|WD=Q11474374}}, {{ill|lt=Nada-ku|Q1154555|WD=Q1154555}}, {{ill|lt=Kobe|Q48320|WD=Q48320}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029}} was prefectural shrine. It stands on a hill north of where {{ill|lt=National Route 2|Q1131340|WD=Q1131340}} and {{ill|lt=National Route 43|Q6157520|WD=Q6157520}} run together east-west. About 150 m southeast of {{ill|lt=Iwaya Station (Hyōgo)|Q6100854|WD=Q6100854|lt=Iwaya Station}} on Hanshin. === Enshrined deity === In old writings, Minume was also written as 汶売, 美奴売, 三犬女, 見宿女, etc. Now the main deity is {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, with {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Kōtaishin}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kumano no Imasu Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} as associated deities; until the Edo period it was called “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gozu Tennō|1=UNKNOWN}}.” The original deity was Minume-no-Kami (美奴売神 / 敏馬神). From the name, Minume-no-Kami is considered the same as the water deity {{ill|lt=Mizuhano-me||WD=|lt=Mitsuha-no-me-no-Kami}}, now enshrined in the precinct water shrine. There is a well called Akai or Miro-me Shimizu. The view that this goddess gave her name to Minume is considered strongest. Another theory, likely inspired by {{ill|lt=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Q380818|WD=Q380818}}’s poem “Passing Minume where seaweed is harvested...,” derives it from miru ({{ill|lt=Miru seaweed|Q2712208|WD=Q2712208|lt=miru}}), used in making sea-salt. === History === An excerpt of ''Settsu no Kuni Fudoki'' records this shrine’s foundation. When {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803}} departed for conquest of Silla, she prayed for victory at Kamisaki Matsubara in {{ill|lt=Kawabe District|Q1042053|WD=Q1042053}} (present {{ill|lt=Kanzaki (Amagasaki)|Q11589609|WD=Q11589609|lt=Kanzaki}} in {{ill|lt=Amagasaki|Q213318|WD=Q213318}} or {{ill|lt=Toyonaka|Q467479|WD=Q467479}}). A deity from Minume-yama in Nose upstream on the {{ill|lt=Inagawa|Q11571867|WD=Q11571867}} told her to cut cedars from Minume-yama (Mikusa-yama in present Toyono District, Osaka) and make ships to ensure victory. On return, ships stopped offshore of this place, then a cape in antiquity; divination revealed it was the will of the Minume-yama deity, so Minume-no-Kami was enshrined here. This appears to preserve some historical truth. The ''{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki|Q11381815|WD=Q11381815}}'' also records the female deities of Inagawa and {{ill|lt=Muko River|Q6933569|WD=Q6933569}} competing for favor of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sumiyoshi Ōkami|1=UNKNOWN}}. Another ''Settsu no Kuni Fudoki'' excerpt says that before {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Toyouke Ōkami|1=UNKNOWN}} moved to {{ill|lt=Tanba Province|Q954511|WD=Q954511}}, the deity resided at Inakura-yama in Settsu (location unknown), and one theory places this in the upper Inagawa area ({{ill|lt=Toyono District|Q1205516|WD=Q1205516}}?). In the {{ill|lt=Genba-ryō|Q11572314|WD=Q11572314}} section of the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}'', it is noted that when guests from {{ill|lt=Silla|Q28456|WD=Q28456}} visited, sake brewed at {{ill|lt=Ikuta Shrine|Q710086|WD=Q710086}} was served here. Since sake was also served at the {{ill|lt=Kōrōkan|Q11675677|WD=Q11675677}} in {{ill|lt=Naniwa|Q1075406|WD=Q1075406}}, serving sake here was a kind of ritual. In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}'', it is listed as “Minume Shrine, Yatabe District, Settsu Province,” ranked as a small shrine. Because this was the first overnight harbor when traveling by ship westward from the capital through present {{ill|lt=Osaka|Q11439906|WD=Q11439906}}, and also where {{ill|lt=Mount Ikoma|Q996259|WD=Q996259}} and other views recalling {{ill|lt=Yamato|Q13417196|WD=Q13417196}} emerged on return from the west, many poets - including {{ill|lt=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Q380818|WD=Q380818}}, {{ill|lt=Ōtomo no Tabito|Q386287|WD=Q386287}}, and {{ill|lt=Tanabe no Sakimaro|Q11577372|WD=Q11577372}} - composed {{ill|lt=waka|Q737148|WD=Q737148}} here. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, haikai poet {{ill|lt=Yosa Buson|Q31523|WD=Q31523}} also visited repeatedly on his way to the Kitakaze house in Hyōgo. Facing the {{ill|lt=Saigoku Kaidō|Q10518413|WD=Q10518413}} (Hama Kaidō), with various amusements, it was lively with worshippers from land and sea. In August Meiji 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}) it was ranked village shrine; in September Shōwa 5 it was promoted to prefectural shrine. On June 5, Shōwa 20, shrine buildings were destroyed in war damage; rebuilt in Shōwa 27. There used to be a spring beneath the hill of the main sanctuary, but it dried up in the {{ill|lt=Great Hanshin-Awaji Earthquake|Q11392882|WD=Q11392882}}. === God of severing ties === [[画像:Mirume-Jinja-mae-BusStop-20230504.jpg|thumb|200px|Nearest bus stop is read as “'''mirume''' jinja-mae”]] Traditionally, bridal processions do not pass in front of this shrine for fear of divorce. It is said the goddess would be jealous, and the shrine is also known for rites of severing ties. === Reading of “Minume” === As a shrine name, 敏馬 is read “mi'''nu'''me,” but the nearby Hanshin bus stops “Minume Jinja-mae” and “Minume” are read “mi'''ru'''me.” Meiji-Taishō documents show both readings{{Cite book|和書 |title=Latest Kinki Excursion Guide |date=1911-07|publisher=Naniwa Shobō |pages=Hanshin Electric Railway line 22 |author=Edited by Naniwa-kai}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Landmarks |date=1897-10|publisher=Kumagai Kueidō |page=7 |author=Kadokawa Monzō (Takagiyama-jin)}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Centered on Kobe, Part 1 |year=1919|publisher=Akanishi Manyūdō |author=Edited by Harumichi Anji and Senjirō Fukuhara}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Man'yōshū Ryakukai 6 |year=1875|publisher=Katano Tōshirō |page=17}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Scenic Places: with nearby area |date=1897-04|publisher=Nittōkan |page=14 |author=Edited by Naoomi Nabeshima}}. === Notes === {{reflist}} === External links === * {{Instagram|minume_jinja}} * [https://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6301022.html Minume Shrine] - Hyogo Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines {{DEFAULTSORT:みぬめしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Minume Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Minume Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:神戸市の神社]] [[Category:灘区の建築物]] [[Category:摂津国の式内社]] [[Category:兵庫県の旧県社]] [[Category:祇園神社]] [[Category:灘区の歴史]] [[Category:岩屋 (神戸市)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

The ''{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki|Q11381815|WD=Q11381815}}'' also records the female deities of Inagawa and {{ill|lt=Muko River|Q6933569|WD=Q6933569}} competing for favor of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sumiyoshi Ōkami|1=UNKNOWN}}. Another ''Settsu no Kuni Fudoki'' excerpt says that before {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Toyouke Ōkami|1=UNKNOWN}} moved to {{ill|lt=Tanba Province|Q954511|WD=Q954511}}, the deity resided at Inakura-yama in Settsu (location unknown), and one theory places this in the upper Inagawa area ({{ill|lt=Toyono District|Q1205516|WD=Q1205516}}?). In the {{ill|lt=Genba-ryō|Q11572314|WD=Q11572314}} section of the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}'', it is noted that when guests from {{ill|lt=Silla|Q28456|WD=Q28456}} visited, sake brewed at {{ill|lt=Ikuta Shrine|Q710086|WD=Q710086}} was served here. Since sake was also served at the {{ill|lt=Kōrōkan|Q11675677|WD=Q11675677}} in {{ill|lt=Naniwa|Q1075406|WD=Q1075406}}, serving sake here was a kind of ritual. In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}'', it is listed as “Minume Shrine, Yatabe District, Settsu Province,” ranked as a small shrine. Because this was the first overnight harbor when traveling by ship westward from the capital through present {{ill|lt=Osaka|Q11439906|WD=Q11439906}}, and also where {{ill|lt=Mount Ikoma|Q996259|WD=Q996259}} and other views recalling {{ill|lt=Yamato|Q13417196|WD=Q13417196}} emerged on return from the west, many poets - including {{ill|lt=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Q380818|WD=Q380818}}, {{ill|lt=Ōtomo no Tabito|Q386287|WD=Q386287}}, and {{ill|lt=Tanabe no Sakimaro|Q11577372|WD=Q11577372}} - composed {{ill|lt=waka|Q737148|WD=Q737148}} here. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, haikai poet {{ill|lt=Yosa Buson|Q31523|WD=Q31523}} also visited repeatedly on his way to the Kitakaze house in Hyōgo. Facing the {{ill|lt=Saigoku Kaidō|Q10518413|WD=Q10518413}} (Hama Kaidō), with various amusements, it was lively with worshippers from land and sea. In August Meiji 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}) it was ranked village shrine; in September Shōwa 5 it was promoted to prefectural shrine. On June 5, Shōwa 20, shrine buildings were destroyed in war damage; rebuilt in Shōwa 27. There used to be a spring beneath the hill of the main sanctuary, but it dried up in the {{ill|lt=Great Hanshin-Awaji Earthquake|Q11392882|WD=Q11392882}}. === God of severing ties === [[画像:Mirume-Jinja-mae-BusStop-20230504.jpg|thumb|200px|Nearest bus stop is read as “'''mirume''' jinja-mae”]] Traditionally, bridal processions do not pass in front of this shrine for fear of divorce. It is said the goddess would be jealous, and the shrine is also known for rites of severing ties. === Reading of “Minume” === As a shrine name, 敏馬 is read “mi'''nu'''me,” but the nearby Hanshin bus stops “Minume Jinja-mae” and “Minume” are read “mi'''ru'''me.” Meiji-Taishō documents show both readings{{Cite book|和書 |title=Latest Kinki Excursion Guide |date=1911-07|publisher=Naniwa Shobō |pages=Hanshin Electric Railway line 22 |author=Edited by Naniwa-kai}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Landmarks |date=1897-10|publisher=Kumagai Kueidō |page=7 |author=Kadokawa Monzō (Takagiyama-jin)}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Centered on Kobe, Part 1 |year=1919|publisher=Akanishi Manyūdō |author=Edited by Harumichi Anji and Senjirō Fukuhara}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Man'yōshū Ryakukai 6 |year=1875|publisher=Katano Tōshirō |page=17}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Scenic Places: with nearby area |date=1897-04|publisher=Nittōkan |page=14 |author=Edited by Naoomi Nabeshima}}. === Notes === {{reflist}} === External links === * {{Instagram|minume_jinja}} * [https://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6301022.html Minume Shrine] - Hyogo Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines {{DEFAULTSORT:みぬめしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Minume Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Minume Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:神戸市の神社]] [[Category:灘区の建築物]] [[Category:摂津国の式内社]] [[Category:兵庫県の旧県社]] [[Category:祇園神社]] [[Category:灘区の歴史]] [[Category:岩屋 (神戸市)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]
