The ''{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki|Q11381815|WD=Q11381815|1=Sumiyoshi Taisha Shrine Chronicles}}'' also records the female deities of Inagawa and {{ill|lt=Muko River|Q6933569|WD=Q6933569|1=Muko River}} competing for favor of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sumiyoshi Ōkami|WD=Q1114013|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}}. Another ''Settsu no Kuni Fudoki'' excerpt says that before {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Toyouke Ōkami|1=UNKNOWN}} moved to {{ill|lt=Tanba Province|Q954511|WD=Q954511|1=Tanba Province}}, the deity resided at Inakura-yama in Settsu (location unknown), and one theory places this in the upper Inagawa area ({{ill|lt=Toyono District|Q1205516|WD=Q1205516|1=Toyono District, Osaka}}?). In the {{ill|lt=Genba-ryō|Q11572314|WD=Q11572314|1=Genba-ryō}} section of the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'', it is noted that when guests from {{ill|lt=Silla|Q28456|WD=Q28456|1=Silla}} visited, sake brewed at {{ill|lt=Ikuta Shrine|Q710086|WD=Q710086|1=Ikuta Shrine}} was served here. Since sake was also served at the {{ill|lt=Kōrōkan|Q11675677|WD=Q11675677|1=Kōrokan}} in {{ill|lt=Naniwa|Q1075406|WD=Q1075406|1=Namba}}, serving sake here was a kind of ritual. In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'', it is listed as “Minume Shrine, Yatabe District, Settsu Province,” ranked as a small shrine. Because this was the first overnight harbor when traveling by ship westward from the capital through present {{ill|lt=Osaka|Q11439906|WD=Q11439906|1=Ōsaka}}, and also where {{ill|lt=Mount Ikoma|Q996259|WD=Q996259|1=Mount Ikoma}} and other views recalling {{ill|lt=Yamato|Q13417196|WD=Q13417196|1=Yamato}} emerged on return from the west, many poets - including {{ill|lt=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Q380818|WD=Q380818|1=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro}}, {{ill|lt=Ōtomo no Tabito|Q386287|WD=Q386287|1=Ōtomo no Tabito}}, and {{ill|lt=Tanabe no Sakimaro|Q11577372|WD=Q11577372|1=Tanabe no Sakimaro}} - composed {{ill|lt=waka|Q737148|WD=Q737148|1=Waka (poetry)}} here. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, haikai poet {{ill|lt=Yosa Buson|Q31523|WD=Q31523|1=Yosa Buson}} also visited repeatedly on his way to the Kitakaze house in Hyōgo. Facing the {{ill|lt=Saigoku Kaidō|Q10518413|WD=Q10518413|1=Seigoku kaidō}} (Hama Kaidō), with various amusements, it was lively with worshippers from land and sea. In August Meiji 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}) it was ranked village shrine; in September Shōwa 5 it was promoted to prefectural shrine. On June 5, Shōwa 20, shrine buildings were destroyed in war damage; rebuilt in Shōwa 27. There used to be a spring beneath the hill of the main sanctuary, but it dried up in the {{ill|lt=Great Hanshin-Awaji Earthquake|Q11392882|WD=Q11392882|1=Damage by Great Hanshin earthquake}}. === God of severing ties === [[画像:Mirume-Jinja-mae-BusStop-20230504.jpg|thumb|200px|Nearest bus stop is read as “'''mirume''' jinja-mae”]] Traditionally, bridal processions do not pass in front of this shrine for fear of divorce. It is said the goddess would be jealous, and the shrine is also known for rites of severing ties. === Reading of “Minume” === As a shrine name, 敏馬 is read “mi'''nu'''me,” but the nearby Hanshin bus stops “Minume Jinja-mae” and “Minume” are read “mi'''ru'''me.” Meiji-Taishō documents show both readings{{Cite book|和書 |title=Latest Kinki Excursion Guide |date=1911-07|publisher=Naniwa Shobō |pages=Hanshin Electric Railway line 22 |author=Edited by Naniwa-kai}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Landmarks |date=1897-10|publisher=Kumagai Kueidō |page=7 |author=Kadokawa Monzō (Takagiyama-jin)}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Centered on Kobe, Part 1 |year=1919|publisher=Akanishi Manyūdō |author=Edited by Harumichi Anji and Senjirō Fukuhara}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Man'yōshū Ryakukai 6 |year=1875|publisher=Katano Tōshirō |page=17}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Scenic Places: with nearby area |date=1897-04|publisher=Nittōkan |page=14 |author=Edited by Naoomi Nabeshima}}. === Notes === {{reflist}} === External links === * {{Instagram|minume_jinja}} * [https://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6301022.html Minume Shrine] - Hyogo Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Minume Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Minume Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:神戸市の神社]] [[Category:Buildings in Nada-ku, Kobe]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:祇園神社]] [[Category:灘区の歴史]] [[Category:岩屋 (神戸市)]][[Category:qq]]

Shrine | 画像=[[ファイル:Minume shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kobe|Q48320|WD=Q48320|1=Kobe}}{{ill|lt=Nada-ku|Q1154555|WD=Q1154555|1=Nada-ku, Kobe}} Iwaya Naka-machi 4-1-8 | 位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} | 創建={{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} 1 | 本殿={{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} | 例祭={{ill|lt=October 10|Q2921|WD=Q2921|1=October 10}} | 地図 = {{Location map|Kobe|label=Minume Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Minume Shrine''' (みぬめじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Iwaya Naka-machi|Q11474374|WD=Q11474374|1=Iwaya Nakamachi}}, {{ill|lt=Nada-ku|Q1154555|WD=Q1154555|1=Nada-ku, Kobe}}, {{ill|lt=Kobe|Q48320|WD=Q48320|1=Kobe}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was prefectural shrine. It stands on a hill north of where {{ill|lt=National Route 2|Q1131340|WD=Q1131340|1=Japan National Route 2}} and {{ill|lt=National Route 43|Q6157520|WD=Q6157520|1=Japan National Route 43}} run together east-west. About 150 m southeast of {{ill|lt=Iwaya Station (Hyōgo)|Q6100854|WD=Q6100854|lt=Iwaya Station|1=Iwaya Station (Hyōgo)}} on Hanshin. === Enshrined deity === In old writings, Minume was also written as 汶売, 美奴売, 三犬女, 見宿女, etc. Now the main deity is {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, with {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Kōtaishin|1=Amaterasu}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kumano no Imasu Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} as associated deities; until the Edo period it was called “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gozu Tennō|1=UNKNOWN}}.” The original deity was Minume-no-Kami (美奴売神 / 敏馬神). From the name, Minume-no-Kami is considered the same as the water deity {{ill|lt=Mizuhano-me|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitsuha-no-me-no-Kami}}, now enshrined in the precinct water shrine. There is a well called Akai or Miro-me Shimizu. The view that this goddess gave her name to Minume is considered strongest. Another theory, likely inspired by {{ill|lt=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Q380818|WD=Q380818|1=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro}}’s poem “Passing Minume where seaweed is harvested...,” derives it from miru ({{ill|lt=Miru seaweed|Q2712208|WD=Q2712208|lt=miru|1=Codium fragile}}), used in making sea-salt. === History === An excerpt of ''Settsu no Kuni Fudoki'' records this shrine’s foundation. When {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} departed for conquest of Silla, she prayed for victory at Kamisaki Matsubara in {{ill|lt=Kawabe District|Q1042053|WD=Q1042053|1=Kawabe District, Hyōgo}} (present {{ill|lt=Kanzaki (Amagasaki)|Q11589609|WD=Q11589609|lt=Kanzaki|1=Kanzaki}} in {{ill|lt=Amagasaki|Q213318|WD=Q213318|1=Amagasaki}} or {{ill|lt=Toyonaka|Q467479|WD=Q467479|1=Toyonaka}}). A deity from Minume-yama in Nose upstream on the {{ill|lt=Inagawa|Q11571867|WD=Q11571867|1=Ina River}} told her to cut cedars from Minume-yama (Mikusa-yama in present Toyono District, Osaka) and make ships to ensure victory. On return, ships stopped offshore of this place, then a cape in antiquity; divination revealed it was the will of the Minume-yama deity, so Minume-no-Kami was enshrined here. This appears to preserve some historical truth. The ''{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki|Q11381815|WD=Q11381815|1=Sumiyoshi Taisha Shrine Chronicles}}'' also records the female deities of Inagawa and {{ill|lt=Muko River|Q6933569|WD=Q6933569|1=Muko River}} competing for favor of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sumiyoshi Ōkami|WD=Q1114013|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}}. Another ''Settsu no Kuni Fudoki'' excerpt says that before {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Toyouke Ōkami|1=UNKNOWN}} moved to {{ill|lt=Tanba Province|Q954511|WD=Q954511|1=Tanba Province}}, the deity resided at Inakura-yama in Settsu (location unknown), and one theory places this in the upper Inagawa area ({{ill|lt=Toyono District|Q1205516|WD=Q1205516|1=Toyono District, Osaka}}?). In the {{ill|lt=Genba-ryō|Q11572314|WD=Q11572314|1=Genba-ryō}} section of the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'', it is noted that when guests from {{ill|lt=Silla|Q28456|WD=Q28456|1=Silla}} visited, sake brewed at {{ill|lt=Ikuta Shrine|Q710086|WD=Q710086|1=Ikuta Shrine}} was served here. Since sake was also served at the {{ill|lt=Kōrōkan|Q11675677|WD=Q11675677|1=Kōrokan}} in {{ill|lt=Naniwa|Q1075406|WD=Q1075406|1=Namba}}, serving sake here was a kind of ritual. In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'', it is listed as “Minume Shrine, Yatabe District, Settsu Province,” ranked as a small shrine. Because this was the first overnight harbor when traveling by ship westward from the capital through present {{ill|lt=Osaka|Q11439906|WD=Q11439906|1=Ōsaka}}, and also where {{ill|lt=Mount Ikoma|Q996259|WD=Q996259|1=Mount Ikoma}} and other views recalling {{ill|lt=Yamato|Q13417196|WD=Q13417196|1=Yamato}} emerged on return from the west, many poets - including {{ill|lt=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Q380818|WD=Q380818|1=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro}}, {{ill|lt=Ōtomo no Tabito|Q386287|WD=Q386287|1=Ōtomo no Tabito}}, and {{ill|lt=Tanabe no Sakimaro|Q11577372|WD=Q11577372|1=Tanabe no Sakimaro}} - composed {{ill|lt=waka|Q737148|WD=Q737148|1=Waka (poetry)}} here. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, haikai poet {{ill|lt=Yosa Buson|Q31523|WD=Q31523|1=Yosa Buson}} also visited repeatedly on his way to the Kitakaze house in Hyōgo. Facing the {{ill|lt=Saigoku Kaidō|Q10518413|WD=Q10518413|1=Seigoku kaidō}} (Hama Kaidō), with various amusements, it was lively with worshippers from land and sea. In August Meiji 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}) it was ranked village shrine; in September Shōwa 5 it was promoted to prefectural shrine. On June 5, Shōwa 20, shrine buildings were destroyed in war damage; rebuilt in Shōwa 27. There used to be a spring beneath the hill of the main sanctuary, but it dried up in the {{ill|lt=Great Hanshin-Awaji Earthquake|Q11392882|WD=Q11392882|1=Damage by Great Hanshin earthquake}}. === God of severing ties === [[画像:Mirume-Jinja-mae-BusStop-20230504.jpg|thumb|200px|Nearest bus stop is read as “'''mirume''' jinja-mae”]] Traditionally, bridal processions do not pass in front of this shrine for fear of divorce. It is said the goddess would be jealous, and the shrine is also known for rites of severing ties. === Reading of “Minume” === As a shrine name, 敏馬 is read “mi'''nu'''me,” but the nearby Hanshin bus stops “Minume Jinja-mae” and “Minume” are read “mi'''ru'''me.” Meiji-Taishō documents show both readings{{Cite book|和書 |title=Latest Kinki Excursion Guide |date=1911-07|publisher=Naniwa Shobō |pages=Hanshin Electric Railway line 22 |author=Edited by Naniwa-kai}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Landmarks |date=1897-10|publisher=Kumagai Kueidō |page=7 |author=Kadokawa Monzō (Takagiyama-jin)}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Centered on Kobe, Part 1 |year=1919|publisher=Akanishi Manyūdō |author=Edited by Harumichi Anji and Senjirō Fukuhara}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Man'yōshū Ryakukai 6 |year=1875|publisher=Katano Tōshirō |page=17}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Scenic Places: with nearby area |date=1897-04|publisher=Nittōkan |page=14 |author=Edited by Naoomi Nabeshima}}. === Notes === {{reflist}} === External links === * {{Instagram|minume_jinja}} * [https://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6301022.html Minume Shrine] - Hyogo Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Minume Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Minume Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kobe]] [[Category:Buildings in Nada-ku, Kobe]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:祇園神社]] [[Category:灘区の歴史]] [[Category:岩屋 (神戸市)]][[Category:qq]]

8140005000148 == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '兵庫県神戸市灘区岩屋中町4-1-8', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:敏馬神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11499217}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=April 2020}} {{神社 | 名称=Minume Shrine | 画像=[[ファイル:Minume shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kobe|Q48320|WD=Q48320|1=Kobe}}{{ill|lt=Nada-ku|Q1154555|WD=Q1154555|1=Nada-ku, Kobe}} Iwaya Naka-machi 4-1-8 | 位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} | 創建={{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} 1 | 本殿={{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} | 例祭={{ill|lt=October 10|Q2921|WD=Q2921|1=October 10}} | 地図 = {{Location map|Kobe|label=Minume Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Minume Shrine''' (みぬめじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Iwaya Naka-machi|Q11474374|WD=Q11474374|1=Iwaya Nakamachi}}, {{ill|lt=Nada-ku|Q1154555|WD=Q1154555|1=Nada-ku, Kobe}}, {{ill|lt=Kobe|Q48320|WD=Q48320|1=Kobe}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was prefectural shrine. It stands on a hill north of where {{ill|lt=National Route 2|Q1131340|WD=Q1131340|1=Japan National Route 2}} and {{ill|lt=National Route 43|Q6157520|WD=Q6157520|1=Japan National Route 43}} run together east-west. About 150 m southeast of {{ill|lt=Iwaya Station (Hyōgo)|Q6100854|WD=Q6100854|lt=Iwaya Station|1=Iwaya Station (Hyōgo)}} on Hanshin. === Enshrined deity === In old writings, Minume was also written as 汶売, 美奴売, 三犬女, 見宿女, etc. Now the main deity is {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, with {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Kōtaishin|1=Amaterasu}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kumano no Imasu Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} as associated deities; until the Edo period it was called “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gozu Tennō|1=UNKNOWN}}.” The original deity was Minume-no-Kami (美奴売神 / 敏馬神). From the name, Minume-no-Kami is considered the same as the water deity {{ill|lt=Mizuhano-me|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitsuha-no-me-no-Kami}}, now enshrined in the precinct water shrine. There is a well called Akai or Miro-me Shimizu. The view that this goddess gave her name to Minume is considered strongest. Another theory, likely inspired by {{ill|lt=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Q380818|WD=Q380818|1=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro}}’s poem “Passing Minume where seaweed is harvested...,” derives it from miru ({{ill|lt=Miru seaweed|Q2712208|WD=Q2712208|lt=miru|1=Codium fragile}}), used in making sea-salt. === History === An excerpt of ''Settsu no Kuni Fudoki'' records this shrine’s foundation. When {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} departed for conquest of Silla, she prayed for victory at Kamisaki Matsubara in {{ill|lt=Kawabe District|Q1042053|WD=Q1042053|1=Kawabe District, Hyōgo}} (present {{ill|lt=Kanzaki (Amagasaki)|Q11589609|WD=Q11589609|lt=Kanzaki|1=Kanzaki}} in {{ill|lt=Amagasaki|Q213318|WD=Q213318|1=Amagasaki}} or {{ill|lt=Toyonaka|Q467479|WD=Q467479|1=Toyonaka}}). A deity from Minume-yama in Nose upstream on the {{ill|lt=Inagawa|Q11571867|WD=Q11571867|1=Ina River}} told her to cut cedars from Minume-yama (Mikusa-yama in present Toyono District, Osaka) and make ships to ensure victory. On return, ships stopped offshore of this place, then a cape in antiquity; divination revealed it was the will of the Minume-yama deity, so Minume-no-Kami was enshrined here. This appears to preserve some historical truth. The ''{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki|Q11381815|WD=Q11381815|1=Sumiyoshi Taisha Shrine Chronicles}}'' also records the female deities of Inagawa and {{ill|lt=Muko River|Q6933569|WD=Q6933569|1=Muko River}} competing for favor of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sumiyoshi Ōkami|WD=Q1114013|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}}. Another ''Settsu no Kuni Fudoki'' excerpt says that before {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Toyouke Ōkami|1=UNKNOWN}} moved to {{ill|lt=Tanba Province|Q954511|WD=Q954511|1=Tanba Province}}, the deity resided at Inakura-yama in Settsu (location unknown), and one theory places this in the upper Inagawa area ({{ill|lt=Toyono District|Q1205516|WD=Q1205516|1=Toyono District, Osaka}}?). In the {{ill|lt=Genba-ryō|Q11572314|WD=Q11572314|1=Genba-ryō}} section of the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'', it is noted that when guests from {{ill|lt=Silla|Q28456|WD=Q28456|1=Silla}} visited, sake brewed at {{ill|lt=Ikuta Shrine|Q710086|WD=Q710086|1=Ikuta Shrine}} was served here. Since sake was also served at the {{ill|lt=Kōrōkan|Q11675677|WD=Q11675677|1=Kōrokan}} in {{ill|lt=Naniwa|Q1075406|WD=Q1075406|1=Namba}}, serving sake here was a kind of ritual. In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'', it is listed as “Minume Shrine, Yatabe District, Settsu Province,” ranked as a small shrine. Because this was the first overnight harbor when traveling by ship westward from the capital through present {{ill|lt=Osaka|Q11439906|WD=Q11439906|1=Ōsaka}}, and also where {{ill|lt=Mount Ikoma|Q996259|WD=Q996259|1=Mount Ikoma}} and other views recalling {{ill|lt=Yamato|Q13417196|WD=Q13417196|1=Yamato}} emerged on return from the west, many poets - including {{ill|lt=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Q380818|WD=Q380818|1=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro}}, {{ill|lt=Ōtomo no Tabito|Q386287|WD=Q386287|1=Ōtomo no Tabito}}, and {{ill|lt=Tanabe no Sakimaro|Q11577372|WD=Q11577372|1=Tanabe no Sakimaro}} - composed {{ill|lt=waka|Q737148|WD=Q737148|1=Waka (poetry)}} here. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, haikai poet {{ill|lt=Yosa Buson|Q31523|WD=Q31523|1=Yosa Buson}} also visited repeatedly on his way to the Kitakaze house in Hyōgo. Facing the {{ill|lt=Saigoku Kaidō|Q10518413|WD=Q10518413|1=Seigoku kaidō}} (Hama Kaidō), with various amusements, it was lively with worshippers from land and sea. In August Meiji 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}) it was ranked village shrine; in September Shōwa 5 it was promoted to prefectural shrine. On June 5, Shōwa 20, shrine buildings were destroyed in war damage; rebuilt in Shōwa 27. There used to be a spring beneath the hill of the main sanctuary, but it dried up in the {{ill|lt=Great Hanshin-Awaji Earthquake|Q11392882|WD=Q11392882|1=Damage by Great Hanshin earthquake}}. === God of severing ties === [[画像:Mirume-Jinja-mae-BusStop-20230504.jpg|thumb|200px|Nearest bus stop is read as “'''mirume''' jinja-mae”]] Traditionally, bridal processions do not pass in front of this shrine for fear of divorce. It is said the goddess would be jealous, and the shrine is also known for rites of severing ties. === Reading of “Minume” === As a shrine name, 敏馬 is read “mi'''nu'''me,” but the nearby Hanshin bus stops “Minume Jinja-mae” and “Minume” are read “mi'''ru'''me.” Meiji-Taishō documents show both readings{{Cite book|和書 |title=Latest Kinki Excursion Guide |date=1911-07|publisher=Naniwa Shobō |pages=Hanshin Electric Railway line 22 |author=Edited by Naniwa-kai}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Landmarks |date=1897-10|publisher=Kumagai Kueidō |page=7 |author=Kadokawa Monzō (Takagiyama-jin)}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Centered on Kobe, Part 1 |year=1919|publisher=Akanishi Manyūdō |author=Edited by Harumichi Anji and Senjirō Fukuhara}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Man'yōshū Ryakukai 6 |year=1875|publisher=Katano Tōshirō |page=17}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Scenic Places: with nearby area |date=1897-04|publisher=Nittōkan |page=14 |author=Edited by Naoomi Nabeshima}}. === Notes === {{reflist}} === External links === * {{Instagram|minume_jinja}} * [https://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6301022.html Minume Shrine] - Hyogo Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Minume Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Minume Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kobe]] [[Category:Buildings in Nada-ku, Kobe]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:祇園神社]] [[Category:灘区の歴史]] [[Category:岩屋 (神戸市)]]

8140005000148 == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '兵庫県神戸市灘区岩屋中町4-1-8', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:敏馬神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11499217}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=April 2020}} {{神社 | 名称=Minume Shrine | 画像=[[ファイル:Minume shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kobe|Q48320|WD=Q48320|1=Kobe}}{{ill|lt=Nada-ku|Q1154555|WD=Q1154555|1=Nada-ku, Kobe}} Iwaya Naka-machi 4-1-8 | 位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} | 創建={{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} 1 | 本殿={{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} | 例祭={{ill|lt=October 10|Q2921|WD=Q2921|1=October 10}} | 地図 = {{Location map|Kobe|label=Minume Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Minume Shrine''' (みぬめじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Iwaya Naka-machi|Q11474374|WD=Q11474374|1=Iwaya Nakamachi}}, {{ill|lt=Nada-ku|Q1154555|WD=Q1154555|1=Nada-ku, Kobe}}, {{ill|lt=Kobe|Q48320|WD=Q48320|1=Kobe}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was prefectural shrine. It stands on a hill north of where {{ill|lt=National Route 2|Q1131340|WD=Q1131340|1=Japan National Route 2}} and {{ill|lt=National Route 43|Q6157520|WD=Q6157520|1=Japan National Route 43}} run together east-west. About 150 m southeast of {{ill|lt=Iwaya Station (Hyōgo)|Q6100854|WD=Q6100854|lt=Iwaya Station|1=Iwaya Station (Hyōgo)}} on Hanshin. === Enshrined deity === In old writings, Minume was also written as 汶売, 美奴売, 三犬女, 見宿女, etc. Now the main deity is {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, with {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Kōtaishin|1=Amaterasu}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kumano no Imasu Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} as associated deities; until the Edo period it was called “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gozu Tennō|1=UNKNOWN}}.” The original deity was Minume-no-Kami (美奴売神 / 敏馬神). From the name, Minume-no-Kami is considered the same as the water deity {{ill|lt=Mizuhano-me|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitsuha-no-me-no-Kami}}, now enshrined in the precinct water shrine. There is a well called Akai or Miro-me Shimizu. The view that this goddess gave her name to Minume is considered strongest. Another theory, likely inspired by {{ill|lt=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Q380818|WD=Q380818|1=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro}}’s poem “Passing Minume where seaweed is harvested...,” derives it from miru ({{ill|lt=Miru seaweed|Q2712208|WD=Q2712208|lt=miru|1=Codium fragile}}), used in making sea-salt. === History === An excerpt of ''Settsu no Kuni Fudoki'' records this shrine’s foundation. When {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} departed for conquest of Silla, she prayed for victory at Kamisaki Matsubara in {{ill|lt=Kawabe District|Q1042053|WD=Q1042053|1=Kawabe District, Hyōgo}} (present {{ill|lt=Kanzaki (Amagasaki)|Q11589609|WD=Q11589609|lt=Kanzaki|1=Kanzaki}} in {{ill|lt=Amagasaki|Q213318|WD=Q213318|1=Amagasaki}} or {{ill|lt=Toyonaka|Q467479|WD=Q467479|1=Toyonaka}}). A deity from Minume-yama in Nose upstream on the {{ill|lt=Inagawa|Q11571867|WD=Q11571867|1=Ina River}} told her to cut cedars from Minume-yama (Mikusa-yama in present Toyono District, Osaka) and make ships to ensure victory. On return, ships stopped offshore of this place, then a cape in antiquity; divination revealed it was the will of the Minume-yama deity, so Minume-no-Kami was enshrined here. This appears to preserve some historical truth. The ''{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki|Q11381815|WD=Q11381815|1=Sumiyoshi Taisha Shrine Chronicles}}'' also records the female deities of Inagawa and {{ill|lt=Muko River|Q6933569|WD=Q6933569|1=Muko River}} competing for favor of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sumiyoshi Ōkami|WD=Q1114013|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}}. Another ''Settsu no Kuni Fudoki'' excerpt says that before {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Toyouke Ōkami|1=UNKNOWN}} moved to {{ill|lt=Tanba Province|Q954511|WD=Q954511|1=Tanba Province}}, the deity resided at Inakura-yama in Settsu (location unknown), and one theory places this in the upper Inagawa area ({{ill|lt=Toyono District|Q1205516|WD=Q1205516|1=Toyono District, Osaka}}?). In the {{ill|lt=Genba-ryō|Q11572314|WD=Q11572314|1=Genba-ryō}} section of the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'', it is noted that when guests from {{ill|lt=Silla|Q28456|WD=Q28456|1=Silla}} visited, sake brewed at {{ill|lt=Ikuta Shrine|Q710086|WD=Q710086|1=Ikuta Shrine}} was served here. Since sake was also served at the {{ill|lt=Kōrōkan|Q11675677|WD=Q11675677|1=Kōrokan}} in {{ill|lt=Naniwa|Q1075406|WD=Q1075406|1=Namba}}, serving sake here was a kind of ritual. In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'', it is listed as “Minume Shrine, Yatabe District, Settsu Province,” ranked as a small shrine. Because this was the first overnight harbor when traveling by ship westward from the capital through present {{ill|lt=Osaka|Q11439906|WD=Q11439906|1=Ōsaka}}, and also where {{ill|lt=Mount Ikoma|Q996259|WD=Q996259|1=Mount Ikoma}} and other views recalling {{ill|lt=Yamato|Q13417196|WD=Q13417196|1=Yamato}} emerged on return from the west, many poets - including {{ill|lt=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Q380818|WD=Q380818|1=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro}}, {{ill|lt=Ōtomo no Tabito|Q386287|WD=Q386287|1=Ōtomo no Tabito}}, and {{ill|lt=Tanabe no Sakimaro|Q11577372|WD=Q11577372|1=Tanabe no Sakimaro}} - composed {{ill|lt=waka|Q737148|WD=Q737148|1=Waka (poetry)}} here. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, haikai poet {{ill|lt=Yosa Buson|Q31523|WD=Q31523|1=Yosa Buson}} also visited repeatedly on his way to the Kitakaze house in Hyōgo. Facing the {{ill|lt=Saigoku Kaidō|Q10518413|WD=Q10518413|1=Seigoku kaidō}} (Hama Kaidō), with various amusements, it was lively with worshippers from land and sea. In August Meiji 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}) it was ranked village shrine; in September Shōwa 5 it was promoted to prefectural shrine. On June 5, Shōwa 20, shrine buildings were destroyed in war damage; rebuilt in Shōwa 27. There used to be a spring beneath the hill of the main sanctuary, but it dried up in the {{ill|lt=Great Hanshin-Awaji Earthquake|Q11392882|WD=Q11392882|1=Damage by Great Hanshin earthquake}}. === God of severing ties === [[画像:Mirume-Jinja-mae-BusStop-20230504.jpg|thumb|200px|Nearest bus stop is read as “'''mirume''' jinja-mae”]] Traditionally, bridal processions do not pass in front of this shrine for fear of divorce. It is said the goddess would be jealous, and the shrine is also known for rites of severing ties. === Reading of “Minume” === As a shrine name, 敏馬 is read “mi'''nu'''me,” but the nearby Hanshin bus stops “Minume Jinja-mae” and “Minume” are read “mi'''ru'''me.” Meiji-Taishō documents show both readings{{Cite book|和書 |title=Latest Kinki Excursion Guide |date=1911-07|publisher=Naniwa Shobō |pages=Hanshin Electric Railway line 22 |author=Edited by Naniwa-kai}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Landmarks |date=1897-10|publisher=Kumagai Kueidō |page=7 |author=Kadokawa Monzō (Takagiyama-jin)}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Centered on Kobe, Part 1 |year=1919|publisher=Akanishi Manyūdō |author=Edited by Harumichi Anji and Senjirō Fukuhara}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Man'yōshū Ryakukai 6 |year=1875|publisher=Katano Tōshirō |page=17}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Scenic Places: with nearby area |date=1897-04|publisher=Nittōkan |page=14 |author=Edited by Naoomi Nabeshima}}. === Notes === {{reflist}} === External links === * {{Instagram|minume_jinja}} * [https://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6301022.html Minume Shrine] - Hyogo Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Minume Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Minume Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kobe]] [[Category:Buildings in Nada-ku, Kobe]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:祇園神社]] [[Category:灘区の歴史]] [[Category:岩屋 (神戸市)]]

It stands on a hill north of where {{ill|lt=National Route 2|Q1131340|WD=Q1131340|1=Japan National Route 2}} and {{ill|lt=National Route 43|Q6157520|WD=Q6157520|1=Japan National Route 43}} run together east-west. About 150 m southeast of {{ill|lt=Iwaya Station (Hyōgo)|Q6100854|WD=Q6100854|lt=Iwaya Station|1=Iwaya Station (Hyōgo)}} on Hanshin. === Enshrined deity === In old writings, Minume was also written as 汶売, 美奴売, 三犬女, 見宿女, etc. Now the main deity is {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, with {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Kōtaishin|1=Amaterasu}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kumano no Imasu Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} as associated deities; until the Edo period it was called “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gozu Tennō|1=UNKNOWN}}.” The original deity was Minume-no-Kami (美奴売神 / 敏馬神). From the name, Minume-no-Kami is considered the same as the water deity {{ill|lt=Mizuhano-me|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitsuha-no-me-no-Kami}}, now enshrined in the precinct water shrine. There is a well called Akai or Miro-me Shimizu. The view that this goddess gave her name to Minume is considered strongest. Another theory, likely inspired by {{ill|lt=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Q380818|WD=Q380818|1=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro}}’s poem “Passing Minume where seaweed is harvested...,” derives it from miru ({{ill|lt=Miru seaweed|Q2712208|WD=Q2712208|lt=miru|1=Codium fragile}}), used in making sea-salt. === History === An excerpt of ''Settsu no Kuni Fudoki'' records this shrine’s foundation. When {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} departed for conquest of Silla, she prayed for victory at Kamisaki Matsubara in {{ill|lt=Kawabe District|Q1042053|WD=Q1042053|1=Kawabe District, Hyōgo}} (present {{ill|lt=Kanzaki (Amagasaki)|Q11589609|WD=Q11589609|lt=Kanzaki|1=Kanzaki}} in {{ill|lt=Amagasaki|Q213318|WD=Q213318|1=Amagasaki}} or {{ill|lt=Toyonaka|Q467479|WD=Q467479|1=Toyonaka}}). A deity from Minume-yama in Nose upstream on the {{ill|lt=Inagawa|Q11571867|WD=Q11571867|1=Ina River}} told her to cut cedars from Minume-yama (Mikusa-yama in present Toyono District, Osaka) and make ships to ensure victory. On return, ships stopped offshore of this place, then a cape in antiquity; divination revealed it was the will of the Minume-yama deity, so Minume-no-Kami was enshrined here. This appears to preserve some historical truth. The ''{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki|Q11381815|WD=Q11381815|1=Sumiyoshi Taisha Shrine Chronicles}}'' also records the female deities of Inagawa and {{ill|lt=Muko River|Q6933569|WD=Q6933569|1=Muko River}} competing for favor of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sumiyoshi Ōkami|WD=Q1114013|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}}. Another ''Settsu no Kuni Fudoki'' excerpt says that before {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Toyouke Ōkami|1=UNKNOWN}} moved to {{ill|lt=Tanba Province|Q954511|WD=Q954511|1=Tanba Province}}, the deity resided at Inakura-yama in Settsu (location unknown), and one theory places this in the upper Inagawa area ({{ill|lt=Toyono District|Q1205516|WD=Q1205516|1=Toyono District, Osaka}}?). In the {{ill|lt=Genba-ryō|Q11572314|WD=Q11572314|1=Genba-ryō}} section of the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'', it is noted that when guests from {{ill|lt=Silla|Q28456|WD=Q28456|1=Silla}} visited, sake brewed at {{ill|lt=Ikuta Shrine|Q710086|WD=Q710086|1=Ikuta Shrine}} was served here. Since sake was also served at the {{ill|lt=Kōrōkan|Q11675677|WD=Q11675677|1=Kōrokan}} in {{ill|lt=Naniwa|Q1075406|WD=Q1075406|1=Namba}}, serving sake here was a kind of ritual. In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'', it is listed as “Minume Shrine, Yatabe District, Settsu Province,” ranked as a small shrine. Because this was the first overnight harbor when traveling by ship westward from the capital through present {{ill|lt=Osaka|Q11439906|WD=Q11439906|1=Ōsaka}}, and also where {{ill|lt=Mount Ikoma|Q996259|WD=Q996259|1=Mount Ikoma}} and other views recalling {{ill|lt=Yamato|Q13417196|WD=Q13417196|1=Yamato}} emerged on return from the west, many poets - including {{ill|lt=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Q380818|WD=Q380818|1=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro}}, {{ill|lt=Ōtomo no Tabito|Q386287|WD=Q386287|1=Ōtomo no Tabito}}, and {{ill|lt=Tanabe no Sakimaro|Q11577372|WD=Q11577372|1=Tanabe no Sakimaro}} - composed {{ill|lt=waka|Q737148|WD=Q737148|1=Waka (poetry)}} here. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, haikai poet {{ill|lt=Yosa Buson|Q31523|WD=Q31523|1=Yosa Buson}} also visited repeatedly on his way to the Kitakaze house in Hyōgo. Facing the {{ill|lt=Saigoku Kaidō|Q10518413|WD=Q10518413|1=Seigoku kaidō}} (Hama Kaidō), with various amusements, it was lively with worshippers from land and sea. In August Meiji 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}) it was ranked village shrine; in September Shōwa 5 it was promoted to prefectural shrine. On June 5, Shōwa 20, shrine buildings were destroyed in war damage; rebuilt in Shōwa 27. There used to be a spring beneath the hill of the main sanctuary, but it dried up in the {{ill|lt=Great Hanshin-Awaji Earthquake|Q11392882|WD=Q11392882|1=Damage by Great Hanshin earthquake}}. === God of severing ties === [[画像:Mirume-Jinja-mae-BusStop-20230504.jpg|thumb|200px|Nearest bus stop is read as “'''mirume''' jinja-mae”]] Traditionally, bridal processions do not pass in front of this shrine for fear of divorce. It is said the goddess would be jealous, and the shrine is also known for rites of severing ties. === Reading of “Minume” === As a shrine name, 敏馬 is read “mi'''nu'''me,” but the nearby Hanshin bus stops “Minume Jinja-mae” and “Minume” are read “mi'''ru'''me.” Meiji-Taishō documents show both readings{{Cite book|和書 |title=Latest Kinki Excursion Guide |date=1911-07|publisher=Naniwa Shobō |pages=Hanshin Electric Railway line 22 |author=Edited by Naniwa-kai}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Landmarks |date=1897-10|publisher=Kumagai Kueidō |page=7 |author=Kadokawa Monzō (Takagiyama-jin)}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Centered on Kobe, Part 1 |year=1919|publisher=Akanishi Manyūdō |author=Edited by Harumichi Anji and Senjirō Fukuhara}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Man'yōshū Ryakukai 6 |year=1875|publisher=Katano Tōshirō |page=17}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Scenic Places: with nearby area |date=1897-04|publisher=Nittōkan |page=14 |author=Edited by Naoomi Nabeshima}}. === Notes === {{reflist}} === External links === * {{Instagram|minume_jinja}} * [https://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6301022.html Minume Shrine] - Hyogo Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Minume Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Minume Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kobe]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nada-ku, Kobe]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:祇園神社]] [[Category:灘区の歴史]] [[Category:岩屋 (神戸市)]]

former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226}} | 創建={{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|qid=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} 1 | 本殿={{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|qid=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} | 例祭={{ill|lt=October 10|Q2921|qid=Q2921|1=October 10}} | 地図 = {{Location map|Kobe|label=Minume Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Minume Shrine''' (みぬめじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Iwaya Naka-machi|Q11474374|qid=Q11474374|1=Iwaya Nakamachi}}, {{ill|lt=Nada-ku|Q1154555|qid=Q1154555|1=Nada-ku, Kobe}}, {{ill|lt=Kobe|Q48320|qid=Q48320|1=Kobe}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|qid=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was prefectural shrine. It stands on a hill north of where {{ill|lt=National Route 2|Q1131340|qid=Q1131340|1=Japan National Route 2}} and {{ill|lt=National Route 43|Q6157520|qid=Q6157520|1=Japan National Route 43}} run together east-west. In August Meiji 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|qid=Q7757|1=1873}}) it was ranked village shrine; in September Shōwa 5 it was promoted to prefectural shrine. On June 5, Shōwa 20, shrine buildings were destroyed in war damage; rebuilt in Shōwa 27. There used to be a spring beneath the hill of the main sanctuary, but it dried up in the {{ill|lt=Great Hanshin-Awaji Earthquake|Q11392882|qid=Q11392882|1=Damage by Great Hanshin earthquake}}. === God of severing ties === [[画像:Mirume-Jinja-mae-BusStop-20230504.jpg|thumb|200px|Nearest bus stop is read as “'''mirume''' jinja-mae”]] Traditionally, bridal processions do not pass in front of this shrine for fear of divorce. It is said the goddess would be jealous, and the shrine is also known for rites of severing ties. === Reading of “Minume” === As a shrine name, 敏馬 is read “mi'''nu'''me,” but the nearby Hanshin bus stops “Minume Jinja-mae” and “Minume” are read “mi'''ru'''me.” Meiji-Taishō documents show both readings{{Cite book|和書 |title=Latest Kinki Excursion Guide |date=1911-07|publisher=Naniwa Shobō |pages=Hanshin Electric Railway line 22 |author=Edited by Naniwa-kai}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Landmarks |date=1897-10|publisher=Kumagai Kueidō |page=7 |author=Kadokawa Monzō (Takagiyama-jin)}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Centered on Kobe, Part 1 |year=1919|publisher=Akanishi Manyūdō |author=Edited by Harumichi Anji and Senjirō Fukuhara}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Man'yōshū Ryakukai 6 |year=1875|publisher=Katano Tōshirō |page=17}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Scenic Places: with nearby area |date=1897-04|publisher=Nittōkan |page=14 |author=Edited by Naoomi Nabeshima}}. === Notes === {{reflist}} === External links === * {{Instagram|minume_jinja}} * [https://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6301022.html Minume Shrine] - Hyogo Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Minume Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Minume Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kobe]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nada-ku, Kobe]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:祇園神社]] [[Category:灘区の歴史]] [[Category:岩屋 (神戸市)]]

Shrine | 画像=[[ファイル:Minume shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|qid=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kobe|Q48320|qid=Q48320|1=Kobe}}{{ill|lt=Nada-ku|Q1154555|qid=Q1154555|1=Nada-ku, Kobe}} Iwaya Naka-machi 4-1-8 | 位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|qid=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226}} | 創建={{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|qid=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} 1 | 本殿={{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|qid=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} | 例祭={{ill|lt=October 10|Q2921|qid=Q2921|1=October 10}} | 地図 = {{Location map|Kobe|label=Minume Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Minume Shrine''' (みぬめじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Iwaya Naka-machi|Q11474374|qid=Q11474374|1=Iwaya Nakamachi}}, {{ill|lt=Nada-ku|Q1154555|qid=Q1154555|1=Nada-ku, Kobe}}, {{ill|lt=Kobe|Q48320|qid=Q48320|1=Kobe}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|qid=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was prefectural shrine. It stands on a hill north of where {{ill|lt=National Route 2|Q1131340|qid=Q1131340|1=Japan National Route 2}} and {{ill|lt=National Route 43|Q6157520|qid=Q6157520|1=Japan National Route 43}} run together east-west. About 150 m southeast of {{ill|lt=Iwaya Station (Hyōgo)|Q6100854|qid=Q6100854|lt=Iwaya Station|1=Iwaya Station (Hyōgo)}} on Hanshin. === Enshrined deity === In old writings, Minume was also written as 汶売, 美奴売, 三犬女, 見宿女, etc. Now the main deity is {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|qid=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, with {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|qid=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Kōtaishin|1=Amaterasu}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kumano no Imasu Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} as associated deities; until the Edo period it was called “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gozu Tennō|1=UNKNOWN}}.” The original deity was Minume-no-Kami (美奴売神 / 敏馬神). From the name, Minume-no-Kami is considered the same as the water deity {{ill|lt=Mizuhano-me|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitsuha-no-me-no-Kami}}, now enshrined in the precinct water shrine. There is a well called Akai or Miro-me Shimizu. The view that this goddess gave her name to Minume is considered strongest. Another theory, likely inspired by {{ill|lt=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Q380818|qid=Q380818|1=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro}}’s poem “Passing Minume where seaweed is harvested...,” derives it from miru ({{ill|lt=Miru seaweed|Q2712208|qid=Q2712208|lt=miru|1=Codium fragile}}), used in making sea-salt. === History === An excerpt of ''Settsu no Kuni Fudoki'' records this shrine’s foundation. When {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|qid=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} departed for conquest of Silla, she prayed for victory at Kamisaki Matsubara in {{ill|lt=Kawabe District|Q1042053|qid=Q1042053|1=Kawabe District, Hyōgo}} (present {{ill|lt=Kanzaki (Amagasaki)|Q11589609|qid=Q11589609|lt=Kanzaki|1=Kanzaki}} in {{ill|lt=Amagasaki|Q213318|qid=Q213318|1=Amagasaki}} or {{ill|lt=Toyonaka|Q467479|qid=Q467479|1=Toyonaka}}). A deity from Minume-yama in Nose upstream on the {{ill|lt=Inagawa|Q11571867|qid=Q11571867|1=Ina River}} told her to cut cedars from Minume-yama (Mikusa-yama in present Toyono District, Osaka) and make ships to ensure victory. On return, ships stopped offshore of this place, then a cape in antiquity; divination revealed it was the will of the Minume-yama deity, so Minume-no-Kami was enshrined here. This appears to preserve some historical truth. The ''{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki|Q11381815|qid=Q11381815|1=Sumiyoshi Taisha Shrine Chronicles}}'' also records the female deities of Inagawa and {{ill|lt=Muko River|Q6933569|qid=Q6933569|1=Muko River}} competing for favor of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sumiyoshi Ōkami|qid=Q1114013|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}}. Another ''Settsu no Kuni Fudoki'' excerpt says that before {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Toyouke Ōkami|1=UNKNOWN}} moved to {{ill|lt=Tanba Province|Q954511|qid=Q954511|1=Tanba Province}}, the deity resided at Inakura-yama in Settsu (location unknown), and one theory places this in the upper Inagawa area ({{ill|lt=Toyono District|Q1205516|qid=Q1205516|1=Toyono District, Osaka}}?). In the {{ill|lt=Genba-ryō|Q11572314|qid=Q11572314|1=Genba-ryō}} section of the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|qid=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'', it is noted that when guests from {{ill|lt=Silla|Q28456|qid=Q28456|1=Silla}} visited, sake brewed at {{ill|lt=Ikuta Shrine|Q710086|qid=Q710086|1=Ikuta Shrine}} was served here. Since sake was also served at the {{ill|lt=Kōrōkan|Q11675677|qid=Q11675677|1=Kōrokan}} in {{ill|lt=Naniwa|Q1075406|qid=Q1075406|1=Namba}}, serving sake here was a kind of ritual. In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'', it is listed as “Minume Shrine, Yatabe District, Settsu Province,” ranked as a small shrine. Because this was the first overnight harbor when traveling by ship westward from the capital through present {{ill|lt=Osaka|Q11439906|qid=Q11439906|1=Ōsaka}}, and also where {{ill|lt=Mount Ikoma|Q996259|qid=Q996259|1=Mount Ikoma}} and other views recalling {{ill|lt=Yamato|Q13417196|qid=Q13417196|1=Yamato}} emerged on return from the west, many poets - including {{ill|lt=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Q380818|qid=Q380818|1=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro}}, {{ill|lt=Ōtomo no Tabito|Q386287|qid=Q386287|1=Ōtomo no Tabito}}, and {{ill|lt=Tanabe no Sakimaro|Q11577372|qid=Q11577372|1=Tanabe no Sakimaro}} - composed {{ill|lt=waka|Q737148|qid=Q737148|1=Waka (poetry)}} here. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|qid=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, haikai poet {{ill|lt=Yosa Buson|Q31523|qid=Q31523|1=Yosa Buson}} also visited repeatedly on his way to the Kitakaze house in Hyōgo. Facing the {{ill|lt=Saigoku Kaidō|Q10518413|qid=Q10518413|1=Seigoku kaidō}} (Hama Kaidō), with various amusements, it was lively with worshippers from land and sea. In August Meiji 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|qid=Q7757|1=1873}}) it was ranked village shrine; in September Shōwa 5 it was promoted to prefectural shrine. On June 5, Shōwa 20, shrine buildings were destroyed in war damage; rebuilt in Shōwa 27. There used to be a spring beneath the hill of the main sanctuary, but it dried up in the {{ill|lt=Great Hanshin-Awaji Earthquake|Q11392882|qid=Q11392882|1=Damage by Great Hanshin earthquake}}. === God of severing ties === [[画像:Mirume-Jinja-mae-BusStop-20230504.jpg|thumb|200px|Nearest bus stop is read as “'''mirume''' jinja-mae”]] Traditionally, bridal processions do not pass in front of this shrine for fear of divorce. It is said the goddess would be jealous, and the shrine is also known for rites of severing ties. === Reading of “Minume” === As a shrine name, 敏馬 is read “mi'''nu'''me,” but the nearby Hanshin bus stops “Minume Jinja-mae” and “Minume” are read “mi'''ru'''me.” Meiji-Taishō documents show both readings{{Cite book|和書 |title=Latest Kinki Excursion Guide |date=1911-07|publisher=Naniwa Shobō |pages=Hanshin Electric Railway line 22 |author=Edited by Naniwa-kai}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Landmarks |date=1897-10|publisher=Kumagai Kueidō |page=7 |author=Kadokawa Monzō (Takagiyama-jin)}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Centered on Kobe, Part 1 |year=1919|publisher=Akanishi Manyūdō |author=Edited by Harumichi Anji and Senjirō Fukuhara}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Man'yōshū Ryakukai 6 |year=1875|publisher=Katano Tōshirō |page=17}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Scenic Places: with nearby area |date=1897-04|publisher=Nittōkan |page=14 |author=Edited by Naoomi Nabeshima}}. === Notes === {{reflist}} === External links === * {{Instagram|minume_jinja}} * [https://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6301022.html Minume Shrine] - Hyogo Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Minume Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Minume Shrine]][[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kobe]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nada-ku, Kobe]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:祇園神社]] [[Category:灘区の歴史]] [[Category:岩屋 (神戸市)]]

8140005000148 == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '兵庫県神戸市灘区岩屋中町4-1-8', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:敏馬神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11499217}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=April 2020}} {{神社 | 名称=Minume Shrine | 画像=[[ファイル:Minume shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|qid=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kobe|Q48320|qid=Q48320|1=Kobe}}{{ill|lt=Nada-ku|Q1154555|qid=Q1154555|1=Nada-ku, Kobe}} Iwaya Naka-machi 4-1-8 | 位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|qid=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226}} | 創建={{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|qid=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} 1 | 本殿={{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|qid=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} | 例祭={{ill|lt=October 10|Q2921|qid=Q2921|1=October 10}} | 地図 = {{Location map|Kobe|label=Minume Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Minume Shrine''' (みぬめじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Iwaya Naka-machi|Q11474374|qid=Q11474374|1=Iwaya Nakamachi}}, {{ill|lt=Nada-ku|Q1154555|qid=Q1154555|1=Nada-ku, Kobe}}, {{ill|lt=Kobe|Q48320|qid=Q48320|1=Kobe}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|qid=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was prefectural shrine. It stands on a hill north of where {{ill|lt=National Route 2|Q1131340|qid=Q1131340|1=Japan National Route 2}} and {{ill|lt=National Route 43|Q6157520|qid=Q6157520|1=Japan National Route 43}} run together east-west. About 150 m southeast of {{ill|lt=Iwaya Station (Hyōgo)|Q6100854|qid=Q6100854|lt=Iwaya Station|1=Iwaya Station (Hyōgo)}} on Hanshin. === Enshrined deity === In old writings, Minume was also written as 汶売, 美奴売, 三犬女, 見宿女, etc. Now the main deity is {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|qid=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, with {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|qid=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Kōtaishin|1=Amaterasu}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kumano no Imasu Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} as associated deities; until the Edo period it was called “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gozu Tennō|1=UNKNOWN}}.” The original deity was Minume-no-Kami (美奴売神 / 敏馬神). From the name, Minume-no-Kami is considered the same as the water deity {{ill|lt=Mizuhano-me|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitsuha-no-me-no-Kami}}, now enshrined in the precinct water shrine. There is a well called Akai or Miro-me Shimizu. The view that this goddess gave her name to Minume is considered strongest. Another theory, likely inspired by {{ill|lt=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Q380818|qid=Q380818|1=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro}}’s poem “Passing Minume where seaweed is harvested...,” derives it from miru ({{ill|lt=Miru seaweed|Q2712208|qid=Q2712208|lt=miru|1=Codium fragile}}), used in making sea-salt. === History === An excerpt of ''Settsu no Kuni Fudoki'' records this shrine’s foundation. When {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|qid=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} departed for conquest of Silla, she prayed for victory at Kamisaki Matsubara in {{ill|lt=Kawabe District|Q1042053|qid=Q1042053|1=Kawabe District, Hyōgo}} (present {{ill|lt=Kanzaki (Amagasaki)|Q11589609|qid=Q11589609|lt=Kanzaki|1=Kanzaki}} in {{ill|lt=Amagasaki|Q213318|qid=Q213318|1=Amagasaki}} or {{ill|lt=Toyonaka|Q467479|qid=Q467479|1=Toyonaka}}). A deity from Minume-yama in Nose upstream on the {{ill|lt=Inagawa|Q11571867|qid=Q11571867|1=Ina River}} told her to cut cedars from Minume-yama (Mikusa-yama in present Toyono District, Osaka) and make ships to ensure victory. On return, ships stopped offshore of this place, then a cape in antiquity; divination revealed it was the will of the Minume-yama deity, so Minume-no-Kami was enshrined here. This appears to preserve some historical truth. The ''{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki|Q11381815|qid=Q11381815|1=Sumiyoshi Taisha Shrine Chronicles}}'' also records the female deities of Inagawa and {{ill|lt=Muko River|Q6933569|qid=Q6933569|1=Muko River}} competing for favor of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sumiyoshi Ōkami|qid=Q1114013|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}}. Another ''Settsu no Kuni Fudoki'' excerpt says that before {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Toyouke Ōkami|1=UNKNOWN}} moved to {{ill|lt=Tanba Province|Q954511|qid=Q954511|1=Tanba Province}}, the deity resided at Inakura-yama in Settsu (location unknown), and one theory places this in the upper Inagawa area ({{ill|lt=Toyono District|Q1205516|qid=Q1205516|1=Toyono District, Osaka}}?). In the {{ill|lt=Genba-ryō|Q11572314|qid=Q11572314|1=Genba-ryō}} section of the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|qid=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'', it is noted that when guests from {{ill|lt=Silla|Q28456|qid=Q28456|1=Silla}} visited, sake brewed at {{ill|lt=Ikuta Shrine|Q710086|qid=Q710086|1=Ikuta Shrine}} was served here. Since sake was also served at the {{ill|lt=Kōrōkan|Q11675677|qid=Q11675677|1=Kōrokan}} in {{ill|lt=Naniwa|Q1075406|qid=Q1075406|1=Namba}}, serving sake here was a kind of ritual. In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'', it is listed as “Minume Shrine, Yatabe District, Settsu Province,” ranked as a small shrine. Because this was the first overnight harbor when traveling by ship westward from the capital through present {{ill|lt=Osaka|Q11439906|qid=Q11439906|1=Ōsaka}}, and also where {{ill|lt=Mount Ikoma|Q996259|qid=Q996259|1=Mount Ikoma}} and other views recalling {{ill|lt=Yamato|Q13417196|qid=Q13417196|1=Yamato}} emerged on return from the west, many poets - including {{ill|lt=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Q380818|qid=Q380818|1=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro}}, {{ill|lt=Ōtomo no Tabito|Q386287|qid=Q386287|1=Ōtomo no Tabito}}, and {{ill|lt=Tanabe no Sakimaro|Q11577372|qid=Q11577372|1=Tanabe no Sakimaro}} - composed {{ill|lt=waka|Q737148|qid=Q737148|1=Waka (poetry)}} here. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|qid=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, haikai poet {{ill|lt=Yosa Buson|Q31523|qid=Q31523|1=Yosa Buson}} also visited repeatedly on his way to the Kitakaze house in Hyōgo. Facing the {{ill|lt=Saigoku Kaidō|Q10518413|qid=Q10518413|1=Seigoku kaidō}} (Hama Kaidō), with various amusements, it was lively with worshippers from land and sea. In August Meiji 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|qid=Q7757|1=1873}}) it was ranked village shrine; in September Shōwa 5 it was promoted to prefectural shrine. On June 5, Shōwa 20, shrine buildings were destroyed in war damage; rebuilt in Shōwa 27. There used to be a spring beneath the hill of the main sanctuary, but it dried up in the {{ill|lt=Great Hanshin-Awaji Earthquake|Q11392882|qid=Q11392882|1=Damage by Great Hanshin earthquake}}. === God of severing ties === [[画像:Mirume-Jinja-mae-BusStop-20230504.jpg|thumb|200px|Nearest bus stop is read as “'''mirume''' jinja-mae”]] Traditionally, bridal processions do not pass in front of this shrine for fear of divorce. It is said the goddess would be jealous, and the shrine is also known for rites of severing ties. === Reading of “Minume” === As a shrine name, 敏馬 is read “mi'''nu'''me,” but the nearby Hanshin bus stops “Minume Jinja-mae” and “Minume” are read “mi'''ru'''me.” Meiji-Taishō documents show both readings{{Cite book|和書 |title=Latest Kinki Excursion Guide |date=1911-07|publisher=Naniwa Shobō |pages=Hanshin Electric Railway line 22 |author=Edited by Naniwa-kai}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Landmarks |date=1897-10|publisher=Kumagai Kueidō |page=7 |author=Kadokawa Monzō (Takagiyama-jin)}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Centered on Kobe, Part 1 |year=1919|publisher=Akanishi Manyūdō |author=Edited by Harumichi Anji and Senjirō Fukuhara}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Man'yōshū Ryakukai 6 |year=1875|publisher=Katano Tōshirō |page=17}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=Guide to Kobe Scenic Places: with nearby area |date=1897-04|publisher=Nittōkan |page=14 |author=Edited by Naoomi Nabeshima}}. === Notes === {{reflist}} === External links === * {{Instagram|minume_jinja}} * [https://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6301022.html Minume Shrine] - Hyogo Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Minume Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Minume Shrine]][[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kobe]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nada-ku, Kobe]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:祇園神社]] [[Category:灘区の歴史]] [[Category:岩屋 (神戸市)]]
